Hofnar Riderings.basicInfo: The great migratory wagon-cities of the steppe peoples, following seasonal grass on long predetermined routes—summering in the high northern steppes, wintering in the south, with established encampment sites along the cycle. The Riderings are not a kingdom but a confederation of clans and families whose capital is a moving city of hundreds of vast painted wagons arranged in formation. Clan-matriarchs and patriarchs govern together through elder councils; status is built on lineage, deeds, and standing in trade. The Hofnar are the connective tissue of the continent's economy, trade-neutral by reputation, their caravans moving goods through every frontier and between kingdoms that refuse to deal directly. Hofnar contracts are legendarily detailed and reliably honored, and their word is the standard currency of long-distance trust. The slave trade runs through Hofnar hands alongside grain and wool—a moral fault-line dividing progressive clans who refuse the work from traditional clans who take it as ordinary commerce. No standing army; disputes are settled by the clans themselves. Foreign visitors are treated according to contract and custom, and the Riderings will trade with any power on equal terms.

Cephalen Houses.name: Cephalen Houses
Cephalen Houses.basicInfo: The iridescent maritime empire of the cephalopod peoples, whose half-submerged port-capitals anchor sea trade across Hedoria's entire coast. Cephalen civilization is built at the water's edge in places that move as much by swimming as by walking—canals serve as streets, lower city-floors are deliberately flooded, and upper floors are dry, with the same building serving both elements simultaneously. The Cephalen took their monopoly through blockade and force two centuries ago, and hold it through merchant-house compacts that control every tariff, pilot, and route on open water. Distrusted but indispensable; their ships move everything that goes by sea. Houses are autonomous and politically intricate, coordinating through slow consensus and the long view. The Cephalen are unsentimental about their reputation for deception—their color-shifting skin teaches them from childhood to read emotion and master its projection, and by adulthood the skilled are indistinguishable liars. Other races negotiate with them carefully. Other races usually are maneuvered.

Vorok Consortiums.name: Vorok Consortiums
Vorok Consortiums.basicInfo: The mining houses of Hedoria, operating ore-works in every kingdom that will sign their contracts. Seven great houses and dozens of lesser ones organize Vorok civilization; there is no nation, only consortiums. Principals—senior Voroks who have accumulated sufficient ownership—buy into the share-block and move the house by consensus; incompetent principals lose their stake within years. A Vorok belongs to a house the way citizens of other races belong to kingdoms. The houses maintain breeding compounds adjoining major operations—staffed by women under fixed-term bearing contracts, mostly Quelled from the southern markets though some are women of other races in catastrophic debt. Vorok reproduction is contractual; there are no Vorok women beyond the breeding compounds. The Vorok are the best miners and prospectors on the continent, capable of sensing ore beneath ground in ways foreign engineers cannot match. Relations between houses are competitive and careful—maneuvering and undercutting continuous, but open warfare avoided because it damages the brand. Within a house, men know each other across decades and work together for years, building the loyalties that long association produces.

Threshi Kingdom.name: Threshi Kingdom
Threshi Kingdom.basicInfo: The closed kingdom of the fertile lowlands and river-country, held by a locust-mantis people who breed faster than their farmland can feed them in ordinary years. The kingdom is organized around warlord-houses led by males, with consort-females managing the agricultural side; neither side holds power without the other. The king is always male, chosen by formal challenge or dynastic claim, and no Threshi king has died peacefully. Females lay eggs in massive clutches and plow fields with chitin-shovels; males harvest with sickle-arms and ride to war at season's end when the harvest is in. The unattached male surplus—far exceeding available female land—drives a continuous raid-cycle against human kingdoms, particularly Kelmar. Raids are for territory, for Quelled livestock the Threshi cannot breed themselves, and for simple survival in a land that cannot feed all its mouths. No standing army; each war-band rides as an autonomous force. Foreigners are not permitted past border-zones; trade is minimal. The Threshi are entirely self-sufficient and uninterested in the outside world except as a source of what they lack.

Draklid Plateau.name: Draklid Plateau
Draklid Plateau.basicInfo: The Sun-Scoured Plateau of rust, copper, and ochre sandstone, held by Draklid clan-holds carved directly into cliff-faces in the high desert and broken country. Each clan is its own sovereign nation; the Draklid have never produced a king and find the suggestion insulting. Clans govern through matriarchs or patriarchs with absolute authority within their territory and no standing beyond it. Confederations of blood and oath field combined forces when cooperation is worth the price; the great powers of Hedoria have learned the price of miscalculating a Draklid clan's autonomy. The Draklid are long-lived, calculating, patient in negotiation, and move in longer timeframes than other races. Ancestor-honor culture runs deep; clan-oaths carry across generations. Sand-Gate access rotates by clan agreement on a twelve-year cycle, making foreign contact formal, mediated through clan-broker channels, and slow. The landscape is harsh—days searing, nights bitter cold, water scarce and fiercely held—and the architecture reflects a people who have always understood that home is something one may have to defend. Draklid abroad are known for absolute reliability and scrupulous fairness; their honor travels with them the way other races carry names.

Fernwarg Forest.name: Fernwarg Forest
Fernwarg Forest.basicInfo: The deep forest territories of the wolf-and-fern people, a matriarchal pack-society where females are larger and hold absolute authority through both anatomy and custom. Each pack is led by a pack-mother, and succession passes through the female line; males within the pack hunt, father children, and serve as chosen consorts, but cannot lead. The most ambitious males travel, and Fernwarg males are encountered across the continent as mercenaries, scouts, and oath-bound retainers—tall lean canids with green-fern coats that shift with the seasons from lush summer green to winter dark, polite in the formal manner of someone raised not to assume his standing. The Fernwarg hold the open woodland and forest-edge; the Raknid hold the deep old-growth, and the two have maintained an unwritten forest compact since the Ensoulment—no war in living memory, no overlap in hunting grounds, agreed paths marked at the borderlands. The human kingdoms press constantly at the forest margins, testing borders through logging and hunting encroachment; the Fernwarg respond politically with pack-mother delegations to human courts, difficult negotiators who do not soften their positions or hurry toward compromise. The forest packs have begun quietly coordinating with the Raknid for the first time in a century; neither forest people wants the third soul-war, but both are preparing for it.

Molvar Reach.name: Molvar Reach
Molvar Reach.basicInfo: The tunnel-network and mound-hall culture of the Molvar, a people of round warm hospitality and quiet capability who dwell along the margins of the great forest in earthen settlements concealing intricate underground passages and chambers. Molvar civilization runs at human pace but with its own rhythm; the realm is largely self-sufficient and content to maintain that self-sufficiency. Foreign visitors are received with warm tunnel-hospitality—feasts, host-rituals, slow trust earned through genuine connection rather than contract or politics. The mounds are ancient-seeming despite their modest scale, built to blend into the landscape and take advantage of natural contours. The Molvar themselves are rarely encountered far from home; what travels outward is their reputation for quiet reliability and the occasional artisan or advisor hired for specific work. The character is food-flavored, breath-heavy, earthen; the Molvar move through their territories with the certainty of people who have known them for generations and expect to know them for generations more.

Prime Monasteries.name: Prime Monasteries
Prime Monasteries.basicInfo: The cliff-carved monasteries housing the Prime ancestor-mages, a people older than the Ensoulment itself and keepers of bone-vaults holding the remains of every Prime mage who has ever lived. The greatest monasteries are cut into cliff-faces at the base of high cold mountains, terraced over thousands of years into cities of pillars, banners, and skull-iconography—the bones of ancestors displayed in architecture itself. Governance runs through councils of senior mages; the monasteries are places of preservation, deep knowledge, and the long view. Prime magic is the slow conversation with the dead—a mage invokes specific ancestors to lend will and knowledge to a working, making it suited to healing, divination, untangling curses, and questions that have been answered before by someone whose bones remain accessible. It does not throw fireballs or level walls, but it can predict the course of a fight before it begins, prepare ground so one side has every advantage, and lay the slow curse that ensures even a victorious enemy loses the war that follows. The Primes hold historical peerage with Avenor but have withdrawn from active engagement in the world's current crises, focusing instead on preservation of their archives and preparation for the second Wartide they alone are certain is coming. Most Primes never leave their monastery; the rare traveling monks who do are formidable but reduced, carrying only the portable relics of their closest ancestor-relationships.

Avenor.name: Avenor
Avenor.basicInfo: A wide low river-plain bisected by a slow broad river fed by smaller tributaries running down from the mountains to the north. The land is among the most fertile on the continent: deep loam, long arcs of grain country, vineyards on the lower slopes, orchards along the watercourses, and grazing meadows on the higher ground. Wetlands and reed-marshes line the river's lower reach where it meets the sea. The coastline runs in long sand and shingle beaches broken by tidal estuaries. Inland, the plain rises gradually into rolling foothills before breaking against the mountains at the northern edge.

The Reach.name: The Reach
The Reach.basicInfo: A vast forest of layered character. The margins are open woodland and forest-edge country — scattered birch and oak, brush, fern-heavy clearings where the canopy thins and sunlight reaches the floor. The interior is deep old-growth: closed canopy, damp ground, moss-thick trunks, shadowed valleys where the light never reaches midday strength. River-valleys thread between, cutting wide grass-lined corridors through the trees. Seasonal change is dramatic: lush green in spring and summer, russet and gold through autumn, sparse and dark in winter when the bracken dies back. Game is plentiful — deer, boar, larger forest predators, and the great elk of the river meadows. The Fernwarg hold the open and edge country. The Raknid hold the deep old-growth.
The Reach.hiddenInfo: Pre-Wartide ruins stand in some of the deeper valleys, mostly overgrown.

Hinderance Highlands.name: Hinderance Highlands
Hinderance Highlands.basicInfo: Rough high country at the foot of the great northern mountains. The land is broken and uneven — rocky grassland giving way to bare scree slopes, cut by deep gullies, narrow ravines, and sudden cliff-faces where the underlying stone breaks through. Wind is constant and the weather is hard year-round. Winters are long and severe, with deep snow that lingers into late spring. Summers are short, bright, and cold at night. Tarns and small lakes sit in glacier-cut hollows. Heather, stiff grass, and stunted thorn cover the lower slopes; bare stone and lichen the higher. The country grades upward into the mountains proper. Travel is slow on foot and worse by wagon. Inhabited by the human kingdom of Kelmar, with its capital at Shield's End on the lower steppe.

The Sun-Scoured Plateau.name: The Sun-Scoured Plateau
The Sun-Scoured Plateau.basicInfo: A harsh broken desert of deep sandstone canyons and towering flat-topped plateaus. The stone runs in rust, copper, ochre, and deep gold tones that catch the late-afternoon sun and glow. Days are searing; nights are bitter cold. Water is scarce and where it is known it is fiercely held — seasonal washes at the canyon floors, hidden seeps in the cliffs, the rare deep pool in shadowed defiles. Vegetation is sparse: thorn-scrub, hardy succulents, the occasional ancient juniper clinging to a ledge. Wind has shaped the rock into spires, arches, and slot canyons that cut deep into the plateau. Travel follows old paths along the canyon floors and the high mesa tops; there are no roads. Inhabited by the Draklid in their cliff-cut clan-holds.

Lake Wildering.name: Lake Wildering
Lake Wildering.basicInfo: A great inland lake dominating the region, with a long irregular shoreline of bays, headlands, and reed-fringed shallows. The water is deep and cold at the center and shelves into wide marshy flats at the margins. Smaller lakes and bog-pools dot the surrounding country, connected by slow streams and stretches of fen. Mixed forest of birch, alder, and pine ranges across the dry ground between the wetlands; meadows open where the soil drains better. Waterfowl are abundant. Lake-fish, eel, and freshwater shellfish fill the shallows. Wild country runs hard against the settled shore — much of the back-country is unclaimed in any practical sense and difficult to patrol. Inhabited by the human kingdom of Nadorim, with its capital at the north end of the lake and smaller towns and villages strung along the shoreline.

Whitestone coast.name: Whitestone coast
Whitestone coast.basicInfo: A mixed coastal region named for the chalk cliffs that face the sea along long stretches of the shore. Above the cliffs lie open chalk uplands — short grass, sheep-cropped turf, hawthorn windbreaks, and the occasional standing stone. Below them the shoreline runs in alternating cliffs, shingle beaches, and tidal flats. Inland the country drops into wide coastal wetlands: salt-marsh near the sea, freshwater fen further in, threaded by slow rivers and old drainage channels. Surviving farmland sits on the fen-edges and the chalk uplands. Reed-beds and bog-iron pans run through the marsh interior. At low tide the foundations of flooded villages remain visible along sections of the coast, the relics of dike-works lost in the Wartide. Inhabited by the human kingdom of Udorath.

Shieldcap Mountains.name: Shieldcap Mountains
Shieldcap Mountains.basicInfo: A wild high mountain range. Sheer ridges, broken passes, ice-bound peaks, glacier valleys, and weather that turns hard without warning even in summer. The lower slopes are heavy pine forest and scree; the middle ranges are bare rock and snowfields; the heights are stone and ice. Streams run fast and cold out of every valley. A handful of passes are travelled in season by those with means and guides; most of the range is left alone. Wild predators range the upper passes — great wolves, snow-bears, raptors, and worse. The foothills hold scattered Vorok pit-works under contract with the surrounding kingdoms. The high country is otherwise unsettled.
Shieldcap Mountains.hiddenInfo: Old structures stand in the high country — pre-Wartide ruins, abandoned watch-posts, and at least one site that prospectors will not enter for any contract price.

Impasse.name: Impasse
Impasse.basicInfo: A near-impassable region where the southern arm of the mountains falls into the sea. The terrain is a tangled mess of steep cliff-faces, sea-cut ravines, fractured bedrock, and storm-beaten coastline. Salt spray reaches far inland. There are no roads and no clear passes. A few sheltered coves and defensible upland pockets hold extremely remote villages, trading rarely with the world outside. Wild predators range the rocks and the high broken country. Smugglers know tracks the kingdoms do not. Ruins, some pre-Wartide, stand in the deeper ravines.
Impasse.hiddenInfo: Not all of the ruins are empty.

Long Steppe.name: Long Steppe
Long Steppe.basicInfo: Open grassland running wide and flat under a tall sky. The landscape is rolling rather than featureless — long shallow swells, broad shallow valleys, scattered stands of wind-bent trees in the hollows where water collects. Grass runs from knee-high in the wet spring to shoulder-high by late summer, browning and laying down through autumn into the brown stubble of winter. Streams are infrequent but reliable, fed from the high country to the east, running in cut channels across the plain. Seasonal flowers wash colour across the country in spring. Wind is constant. Game runs to grouse, hare, antelope, and the larger steppe predators. Hofnar wagon-cycles cross the region on long seasonal routes, with established encampment sites along the way.

Deep Steppes.name: Deep Steppes
Deep Steppes.basicInfo: Vast open grassland reaching into the deep interior, wider and wilder than the Long Steppe to the east. The country runs flat to the horizon in most directions, broken only by shallow river valleys and the occasional rocky outcrop. Grass is dense and tall through the warm months, cured to standing hay by autumn, buried deep under snow in winter. Great wild herds — aurochs and proto-horse-folk known as the Far-Herds — range the deep interior in vast moving columns. Wolves and the larger steppe cats follow them. In the summer months the great Hofnar wagon-cities encamp here in formation, hundreds of vast wagons drawn up in long avenues; in winter the wagon-cities have moved south and the country is left to the herds and the weather.

acrobatics.name: acrobatics
acrobatics.description: Tumbling, balance, and quick recovery from a fall or a bad position. Perform tricks on a stage, drop into a chamber through a window, regain footing on an unstable surface, slip a grasp, roll under a swung blade.

athletics.name: athletics
athletics.description: Running, jumping, climbing, swimming, hauling, holding ground. The whole-body physical skill that handles every demand of moving the body or moving things with the body. Spends stamina for sustained effort.

intimidation.name: intimidation
intimidation.description: Using fear, presence, or the implied threat of force to bend an outcome. The drawn weapon that doesn't have to be used, the stare that backs a bandit down, the quiet sentence in a noble's hall that lands harder than a shout. Draklid stillness, Vorok grinding voice, and Fernwarg display are all native modes of this skill.

investigation.name: investigation
investigation.description: Patient examination of a place, a person, or a problem. Find clues, read tracks, follow a thread of conversation back to its source, spot the discrepancy in a story, follow ledgers, read body-flicker on a Cephalen, find the herbal traces of a hidden hedge-witch's practice, work out where a thing has been hidden.

performance.name: performance
performance.description: Holding a room — song, dance, story, instrument, formal ceremony, a sermon at the Hand-shrine, the recital of an ancestor-lineage at a Prime gathering. Useful for coin in a tavern, for diplomacy in a hall, and for the ritual register of any race that takes ceremony seriously.

persuasion.name: persuasion
persuasion.description: Moving people through speech and bearing — argument, reason, charm, flattery, the slow build of a contract negotiation, the bartered favor at a tavern table. Easy where the request aligns with what the target already wants. Hard where it conflicts with their loyalties. Impossible where it asks them to abandon core identity.

survival.name: survival
survival.description: Living off the land — finding food and water, building shelter, making fire, reading weather, judging trail, handling the cold of the Hinderance or the dry of the Plateau. Includes basic plantcraft and the field knowledge a hunter or scout carries. Molvar root-speech is layered on this skill; Vorok stone-sense too.

melee.name: melee
melee.description: Fighting in close quarters with any handheld weapon — sword, spear, axe, mace, club, knife, or anything else taken in hand. The skill that gets used when the fight is at arm's length and steel or wood meets armor or flesh. Easy: striking an unarmed civilian. Medium: a competent soldier. Hard: a veteran or skilled duelist. Very hard: legendary fighter, supernatural opponent, or a Prime warden. Used by every fighter, soldier, mercenary, and guard in the world; not used by wizards, who wield the staff under their own skill.

ranged.name: ranged
ranged.description: Loosing or throwing missiles at a distance — bow, crossbow, sling, javelin, hatchet, knife. The skill that handles the entire family of ranged weapons; choose the kind that fits the moment. Easy: stationary target at short range, no wind. Medium: moving target, line of sight, mild wind. Hard: contested shot, weather, or moving from cover to cover. Very hard: shot through a crowd without hitting allies, or a single arrow that has to count. Used by hunters, archers, scouts, marines.

unarmed.name: unarmed
unarmed.description: Fighting with the body — fists, kicks, knees, elbows, headbutts, grapples, throws, holds. Also handles natural weapons where the race carries them: Draklid claw, Threshi sickle-blade forearms, Fernwarg bite. Useful when disarmed, when a kill is not the goal, or when the moment is too close for steel.

staff-work.name: staff-work
staff-work.description:
The Brotherhood's applied martial magic — body, staff, and working as one close-range practice. Spends mana.

Four domains: striking (driving the staff blow with the working behind it, breaking plate or shield), bracing (holding ground that should have broken — stand a charge, anchor a doorway), warding (hardening self, ally, weapon, or armor for a turn), and motion (calling wind, slowing a volley, stopping a blade in mid-arc, stepping where the ground will not hold).

### The staff is delivery, not discount

The bonded battlestaff is the wizard's *delivery mechanism* for magical damage. The metaphor that runs in the agoge: a poisoned blade kills with a small amount of poison applied at the point of impact. A pot of poison thrown at the enemy mostly spills on the ground. The staff is the blade. The mana is the poison. The strike is what makes it land.

With the staff in hand the working travels through wood and contact and arrives where the wizard meant it to arrive. A small spend through a clean strike does what a much larger spend in raw working could not.

### Without the staff

A wizard separated from his bonded staff is not helpless — the working still leaves him. He can ward himself, slow wind, harden a doorway, step through bad ground, conduct most utility wizardry at modestly reduced effect. What he cannot do is *deliver* — without the staff, the working diffuses on its way to the target, and combat-staff abilities (Hardened Strike, Standing Wall, Stop the Blade, Staff Through Plate) are unavailable until the staff is recovered or rebuilt. Recovery is the doctrinally preferred answer; rebuilding is years of attunement.

A staff-less wizard in active combat is, by Brotherhood reckoning, a man with the spark and a problem. He is sent to the rear, given an apprentice's stick to make a substitute, and watched for whether the cooperative tradition starts to look attractive to him in his diminishment.

### Projection

This skill does not project at distance in ordinary practice — without a focus the working dissipates over distance, and the staff is built for impact at contact. A wizard who tries to throw fire pours mana into something that mostly won't reach. The Brotherhood considers this a doctrinal proof that projection is the witches' line; the truer reason is the focus. With enough mana and a sufficient prepared rite a wizard could in principle throw a working at distance, the way he could in principle do anything that mana can buy. He never does, because the staff is what he was trained around, and the training is the man.

Used by Brothers of Telinor, hedge-wizards, and Avenorian consorts. Brotherhood doctrine calls it 'channeling.' Brothers call it the staff or the work.

witchcraft.name: witchcraft
witchcraft.description:
The witch's tradition. Spends from the reservoir. The skill is bounded by what mana can buy and by what the witch can be taught — not by category. The practical menu falls into five domains; the extreme tier is reachable but expensive and morally weighted.

### The five practical domains

- **Projection**: light, flame, cold, force at distance. Witchlight, conjured flame, conjured cold, the lance of force across a hall.
- **Glamour**: concealment, false impressions, the gaze that slides past. Pass unseen, hold another woman's face for an hour, glamour a small group through a checkpoint.
- **Sight**: scrying, intent-reading, divination of the present and recent past. Distance-sight, read the working on an object, trace a thread of intent.
- **Body**: healing, mending, fevers, contraception, birth. The hedge-witch's daily trade and the College's quiet specialty.
- **Hand**: telekinesis, the moving of weight. Hurl a weapon, lift a beam, drag a door, hold a falling body for a beat.

The same skill covers cantrip and boss-fight finisher; the difference is reservoir spent. A witch's working takes time and focus when she works alone — combat workings are not as quick as a wizard's strike, and a witch caught mid-cast is vulnerable. Cooperative work with a willing wizard partner is faster and bigger.

### The extreme tier

With enough mana, the boundary of what witchcraft can do softens. The College does not teach these workings in ordinary curriculum.

- **Restoration of body**: regrow a lost limb, restore an organ, mend a body the standard work cannot. Cost in the high hundreds; requires Body specialty and time.
- **Resurrection**: bring back a recently dead. Cost in the thousands.
- **Memory alteration**: rewrite a memory in a living person. Cost in the hundreds.
- **Aggregated continental working**: many witches and consorts pooling reservoirs on a single rite, the way Avenoria did. Cost in the thousands or tens of thousands. Continent-shaping. Done once, by Avenoria, against the Wartide.

A witch attempting any of these is doing something the world will notice.

### What sits outside witchcraft regardless of mana

True-naming is Prime work — anchored in ancestor-bone, not witch-mana. A witch cannot learn it because she does not have the lineage. Time does not bend in any tradition; the past does not return.

### Where it is practiced

The Queen's College in Avenor is the premier institution for the craft, with the best curriculum, the supply network of mana-hunters and apothecaries that keeps its graduates filled, and political cover to practice openly. The hedge-witch tradition runs everywhere else: village apothecaries, traveling root-gatherers, mentor-and-apprentice lineages passed in private. A hedge-witch may be narrower than a Collegian, occasionally better in one craft and worse in the rest; the College credential is prestige and access more than monopoly. The College trains a smaller cohort of non-human witches — Draklid, Cephalen, Raknid, the rare freed Quell — using the same reservoir model. Other races (Hofnar, Molvar, Vorok, Threshi, Fernwarg) have no witch tradition. Primes do not become witches. Outside Avenor a witch caught working publicly is arrested, tried, exiled, or quietly killed.

ancestor-work.name: ancestor-work
ancestor-work.description:
The Prime monastic magic — slow, exact conversation with named ancestors, conducted through bone-relics worn at throat, chest, wrists, and belt. Each invocation is a specific dead person doing a specific thing.

Does not spend a mana resource. Each portable relic is its own item, with its own ancestor and its own charges. A Prime carries three or four relics on the road and works through whichever fits the moment — a Healer-line, a True-Namer-line, a Bone-Reader-line, the rare warrior-line. At a monastery with full vault access, the practice opens far wider; on the road, it is what the Prime brought.

Excellent at knowledge, healing, divination, true-naming, untangling curses, and long-time work. Useless for battle-magic — there is no Prime fireball, no Prime lightning. A Prime mage in a fight is slow, considered, and works through what the relics can do.

Black-market grave-robbing of Prime bones exists; the magic does not work for thieves, and the families involved suffer untraceable misfortunes across generations.

craft.name: craft
craft.description: Making and repairing the made things of the world — smithing, carpentry, weaving, leatherwork, masonry, brewing, cooking, instrument-making, bonesetting, herbalism, mana-potion brewing where the practitioner has the knowledge. One skill rather than twelve, because the same patient hands and eye for material run across most trades. The specifics live in description: a smith works iron, a weaver works wool, a healer works herbs and bone. Easier in a proper workshop with good tools; harder with improvised materials.

deception.name: deception
deception.description: Lying convincingly. Holding a false story under questioning, conning a stranger, passing as someone you are not, stealing the word out of an opponent's negotiation. Distinct from persuasion, which is honest moving of people; deception is the working misrepresentation. Cephalen masters of color-discipline are native experts; humans manage by craft. A skilled liar reads which lies will hold and which won't, and invents accordingly.

insight.name: insight
insight.description: Reading people — what they want, what they are hiding, what they are about to do. The opposite face of deception. Cephalen Readers are the unmatched practitioners, trained from childhood to read another Cephalen's chromatophore-flicker; outsiders work from posture, voice, hesitation, the small tells the body can't suppress. A witch's intent-sight with no reservoir behind it.

stealth.name: stealth
stealth.description: Moving unseen, unheard, and unconsidered. Slipping a sentry, holding still in cover, crossing a room behind a guard's back, taking a position above a doorway and being part of the ceiling. Distinct from acrobatics; stealth is the discipline of going where you are not noticed. Fernwarg in fern-country and Raknid in deep wood are native experts.

thievery.name: thievery
thievery.description: The trades of the cutpurse and the housebreaker. Pick a lock, lift a coinpurse, palm a small object off a table, slip a knife from a sheath without the wearer feeling the weight change, defeat a simple trap. Common to drifters, smugglers, courtiers with a particular history, and (occasionally and unexpectedly) hedge-witches who have had to live by it.

alchemy.name: alchemy
alchemy.description:
The handling of essences, distillates, reagents, and the slow chemistry of potion-craft. The closest thing to magic that most non-magical people in Hedoria can practice. An alchemist cannot cast a spell, but they can brew the potion that does the casting for the drinker — healing draughts that mend wounds, mana potions that fill a witch's reservoir, contraceptive draughts, sleeping draughts, smoke that kindles a snare, philtres that ease pain or lift mood.

Most magical reagents are creature-sourced: lampfowl tail-feathers and eggs that take a working better than ordinary material, naia parts that potentiate a brew, shed stagling antlers from the licensed Reach harvests, ingredients off the back of any greater creature a hunter has brought down. Material does not carry mana on its own; it is what the alchemist works the working *into*.

Alchemy is also the discipline that handles the world's surviving magical artifacts. Most enchanted items in Hedoria are pre-Ensoulment — old swords whose edge will not dull, charms that warm a winter cottage, scrolls that hold a single working until they burn. New enchantment is rare and only strong witches manage it. Identifying what an old item does, how to safely activate it, and how to repair it is alchemy's province; old apothecaries who have spent their lives at it command serious prices for the assessment alone.

The Queen's College runs a sanctioned alchemy school for the consort-line preparations and the College's own potion supply. Outside Avenor, alchemy is broadly legal — most kingdoms regulate it loosely — but the brewing of mana potions specifically is a witch-line crime where the kingdom can prove it.

medicine.name: medicine
medicine.description: The hands-on care of bodies — wound-cleaning, bone-setting, fever-management, birth-attending, the slow rebuilding of someone who has come close to dying. Distinct from craft and alchemy. Apothecaries and midwives lean on medicine; Raknid healers are quietly the best practitioners on the continent; many village wisefolk practice medicine and hedge-witchcraft interchangeably without distinguishing for the patient.

lore.name: lore
lore.description: Knowing things about the world that aren't immediately in front of you. Histories of the Wartide and the soul-wars, the lineages of noble houses, the rites of the Hand and the Prime monasteries, the kinds of creatures that haunt the Hollows and what their tracks look like, the names of the seven great Prime monasteries, the contract-customs of the Hofnar Riderings. Scribes, archivists, College witches, and Prime monastic scholars carry the deepest lore; veteran mercenaries and old hunters carry the practical kind.

carnal-arts.name: carnal-arts
carnal-arts.description: The practiced art of seduction, intimacy, and the body's use as instrument of pleasure, leverage, and combat pressure. Covers the working-craft of courtesans and tavern companions, the seductive register that shakes a foe's Willpower in a fight, and the carnal channel by which a witch can fill her reservoir directly from a partner. The last is the older direct-fill method, used in private by hedge-witches without potion access and by the College consort-line tradition; refined witch practice has drifted toward distilled potion-use over the last generation. Bounded by what the body can endure, by the discretion the practice demands, and by the open hostility it meets from the Brotherhood and the harder reform kingdoms.

Random.name: Random
Random.description: Fortune favors the bold. Begin your tale where chance decrees.
Random.storyStart: Generate a story start appropriate to the player's chosen traits. Draw on world lore to create a specific situation in a concrete location. The opening should present clear immediate circumstances while leaving many possible paths forward. Incorporate aspects of the character's background without forcing them into a predetermined narrative.

The Tarkle Infestation.name: The Tarkle Infestation
The Tarkle Infestation.description: A contract brought you to a Fernwarg tunnel-mound plagued by burrowing pests. The tarkles are nesting deep; the harvest is in danger. The locals need the infestation cleared.
The Tarkle Infestation.storyStart: You stand in the lantern-lit tunnel of a Fernwarg garden-mound, the earthen walls around you alive with the frantic chittering of tarkles. The light catches on the chitin-glint of the creatures as they dart between the root-crops, burrowing deeper with each moment. The tunnel is narrow — good for defense, cramped for movement. Village workers stand back from the walls, watching. The infestation is deep and spreading. Direct your approach carefully; the narrow space means the tarkles are contained, but so are you. Clear the vermin and secure the harvest, and this contract will hold.

Lost in the Verdant Reach.name: Lost in the Verdant Reach
Lost in the Verdant Reach.description: Tracking through the Verdant Reach wilderness, you walked into territory claimed by a pack of territorial fernwolves. Now they have you cornered against ancient forest.
Lost in the Verdant Reach.storyStart: You press your back against the massive trunk of a deep-forest tree, breathing hard. Three sleek fernwolves face you across the clearing, flanking to cut off the back trails. You were tracking a hunter who disappeared three days back — tracked her sign through the undergrowth, the disturbed brush, the cold fire-ring. Instead you found their territory. The wolves do not attack yet. They watch. The forest around the clearing is dense and old. Behind you the trunk is solid. Ahead of you is the pack, and behind them the deep forest carries the sound of more movement. You are in the wolves' domain now, and they have made that very clear.

First Contract Hall.name: First Contract Hall
First Contract Hall.description: The contract hall of Avenor's marble city, where specialists gather to read the postings and find work. The morning is alive with possibility.
First Contract Hall.storyStart: You stand in the entrance to the contract hall in Avenor, morning light crossing the floor through tall shuttered windows. The hall is half-full — brokers at corner tables, specialists reading the posting-board along the back wall, the air carrying the low murmur of negotiation and deal-making. A Hofnar broker sits in an alcove, watching the room with the particular attention of someone who knows talent. The postings are fresh this morning; work is open in a dozen directions. You have no name in this city yet, but the road ahead is clear.

Wagon Cycle Departure.name: Wagon Cycle Departure
Wagon Cycle Departure.description: A Hofnar wagon-cycle strikes camp at first light, the season opening, the road north across the Long Steppe calling. You stand ready for the first leg of travel.
Wagon Cycle Departure.storyStart: You stand beside the lead wagon in the cold clear dawn as the wagon-cycle breaks camp. The great wheels are being fitted to their harnesses; the senior driver walks the rig, testing the traces and calling out to her team. The encampment is coming down around you — fabric struck, cookfires scattered into cold ash, belongings lashed tight. The cycle will leave within the hour. The steppe stretches ahead gray and open. This is how the road begins.

Sand-Gate Day.name: Sand-Gate Day
Sand-Gate Day.description: The twelve-year opening of Sand-Gate in formal ceremony. Foreign delegations gather in the sandstone forecourt before the Draklid gates. The road into the Plateau opens today.
Sand-Gate Day.storyStart: You stand in the sandstone forecourt of Sand-Gate, the rust-colored cliff rising above, the forecourt already filling with foreign arrivals — merchants, envoys, freelancers drawn by the opening. The clan-broker who holds the gate-keeping stands formal and measured on the stone ledge, and the ceremony of welcome is beginning. A Draklid voice rings out across the forecourt calling the formal acknowledgment. Behind the gate, twelve years of clan politics and new contracts wait. The gate opens a footpath toward work and complication.

The College Atrium.name: The College Atrium
The College Atrium.description: First day of formal training at the Queen's College in Avenor. The open atrium fills with mistresses and new admissions. The long education in witchcraft begins.
The College Atrium.storyStart: You stand in the open atrium of the Queen's College on the morning of formal admission, the vaulted ceiling above carrying sound in strange echoes. The assembled mistresses stand on the upper terrace, robed and waiting. Other first-years stand clustered nearby — some confident, some pale with the weight of what they have chosen. The mistress-of-admissions calls names from the slate, and her voice carries across the stone. When she speaks your name, you step forward to join the rest of the College. Everything you have worked toward begins now.

Long Wells Inn.name: Long Wells Inn
Long Wells Inn.description: A crossroads inn at the heart of the Long Steppe trade-route. Common room and yard alive with drivers, couriers, freelancers. The posting-board holds half a dozen contracts. Work is everywhere.
Long Wells Inn.storyStart: You sit at a heavy wooden table in the common room of the Long Wells Inn at midmorning. Outside the window, the wagon-yard turns over constantly — harness being fitted, hooves loud on stone, drivers calling to each other. The posting-board on the back wall carries half a dozen fresh contracts. A Hofnar broker sits at a corner table, reading the room as travelers pass in and out. The common room is lively and warm. The road opens in any direction from here.

Lake Quay Morning.name: Lake Quay Morning
Lake Quay Morning.description: First light on a Lake Wildering quay-village. Fishing fleet casting off into the mist. Smoke-house fires venting. The road inland lies open.
Lake Quay Morning.storyStart: You stand on the wooden quay at first light, the water beyond stretching into mist. The fishing fleet is putting out — boats in neat progression, voices calling soft across the water. A smoke-house worker across the water waves while stoking the morning fires, smoke rising thin and gray. The quay is alive with quiet purpose. The road inland opens behind you, or the water lies ahead. This is the moment before the day sets its course.

Marches Cold Dawn.name: Marches Cold Dawn
Marches Cold Dawn.description: First muster on the Kelmari winter line as the season opens. A Threshi warband has crested the ridge. Fifteen minutes, maybe less, before they reach the palisade.
Marches Cold Dawn.storyStart: You stand at a wooden palisade in cold gray dawn, your breath ghosting in the air. The watch-horn has gone — three short blasts carrying across the ridge. Ahead on the ridge a mile out, a Threshi warband is moving hard toward you. Twenty riders on the sky-line, closing fast. The lieutenant is shouting positions. There are no more than fifteen minutes at most before they reach the line. The cold is sharp. Your hands are steady. This is the work the winter line demands.

Dispatch Late.name: Dispatch Late
Dispatch Late.description: Three days into a hard kingdom-dispatch ride with one day left to reach the destination. The message must arrive before the seventh bell at Shield's End. The road ahead passes through a stand of trees the service has lost riders in.
Dispatch Late.storyStart: You ride hard down a long road, the dispatch-pouch strapped under your coat. Three days pushed already, one more day to Shield's End if the road holds and your horse does not fail. The seventh bell deadline is taut in your chest. Ahead the road bends into a pine stand where the dispatch service has lost two riders in the past season. The stand is dark even in daylight. The road narrows. Between here and the seventh bell stands whatever waits in those trees — and the choice whether to ride through or find another way around that costs you time you may not have.

Hofnar.name: Hofnar
Hofnar.description: Equine-humanoid; tall, broad-shouldered, mane-haired, hoof-toed, slight muzzle, some lineages with small temple horns. Males barrel-chested and heavy; females slimmer and faster. Lifted from steppe herds. Live in painted clan-wagon-cities that migrate seasonally — no fixed capitals. Organized by clan, confederated into seven Riderings led by elder councils. The continent's overland traders: neutral by reputation, scrupulously contract-bound, indispensable to everyone, allied with no one. Reliability is identity; a broken contract destroys standing for generations. Participate in the Quelled trade, which is splitting progressive and traditional clans toward an open break.
Hofnar.quirk: Your word is your bond, and others perceive your scrupulous honesty as a sign of deep, ancestral reliability.
abilities: Trade-Tongue Negotiator

Draklid.name: Draklid
Draklid.description: Draklid. Drake-descended; tall, lean, sinewy, digitigrade, long active tail, scales in rust/copper/ochre/bronze (rare black), clan-marked along the flanks. Long narrow muzzle, slit-pupil amber/yellow/red eyes, sweeping fan of horns, hooked claws, small vestigial wings useless for flight but expressive in display and adorned in ceremony. Live in cliff-cut sandstone clan-holds in the high desert and broken plateau. Each clan is sovereign; never been unified, openly hostile to the suggestion. Long-lived (matriarchs into their fifth century). Patient, calculating, formally polite, scrupulously fair, terrifying to cheat. Younger sons travel as mercenaries, bodyguards, and oath-bound retainers. Dress in layered desert silks, gold at throat and wrists, clan sigils at the belt.
Draklid.quirk: Your patient and calculating demeanor makes it unnervingly difficult for others to detect when you are displeased.
abilities: Calculating Gaze

Cephalen.name: Cephalen
Cephalen.description: Cephalopod-descended; humanoid silhouette but soft and near-boneless, can squeeze through gaps a third their size. Pearl-and-slate rubbery skin that involuntarily shifts color with emotion. Slit-pupil eyes, webbed long-fingered hands, scalp-tentacles in place of hair (semi-prehensile, used for fine work). Amphibious. Live in half-submerged coastal cities of mother-of-pearl, coral, and worked shell, with flooded lower floors and canal streets. Color-control is the central cultural discipline: children are transparent, adults hold steady, masters project false emotional states with precision. Took the seas by blockade two centuries ago after the human admiralties tried to subordinate them; control all open-water trade by treaty since. Sly as a Cephalen and Cephalen-honest are continental idioms. They do not deny the reputation.
Cephalen.quirk: Your skin involuntarily ripples with color when you feel strong emotions, often betraying your true thoughts.
abilities: Emotional Projection

Molvar.name: Molvar
Molvar.description: Molvar. Tuber-and-root people; low-slung quadrupedal body with two stubby root-fingered upper arms, dense fibrous flesh under thick rind-skin in browns/ochres/umbers, pig-and-mole face with bright forward eyes and whisker-tendrils. Short-lived (~60 years). Live in brightly-lit tunnel-networks under low earthen mounds in central meadows and forest margins. No ruling class — neighborhood councils, decisions made over long meals. Hedonists: eat, drink, brew, garden, couple prolifically; plumpness admired, fatness more so. Genderless — any two can couple, either or both can produce a spud (planted in family soil, sprouts a child after half a year). Speak to roots and plants — coax tunnels into shape, persuade crops to thrive; sometimes hired by surface farmers. Not warlike, but devastating in tunnel defense — broke human armies in the First Soul-War and have been left alone since. Inter-network defensive pact is unwritten and absolute.
Molvar.quirk: You have a natural, soothing presence that causes nearby plants to thrive and grow slightly faster in your presence.
abilities: Plant Whisperer

Raknid.name: Raknid
Raknid.description: Raknid. Redeemed wartide race — the brood-line that turned during the Ensoulment and fought the rest of the Wartide alongside Avenoria's allies. Spider-derived: tall, slender, pale chitin-plated bodies in ivory, bone-white, soft grey, or glossy black. Two human-set arms plus two long jointed clawed spider-legs descending from the lower body — walk in a four-beat gait. Six eyes (large humanlike upper pair, four smaller lower), small folding chelicerae at the mouth, scalp grows a webbed mantle of pale silken fibers. Soft clicking voices, long-lived (comparable to Draklid). No homeland — their parent-race's high country was scoured and remains uninhabited. Live in scattered small communities of dozens to a few hundred in marginal hill country and forest edges. Most Hedorians do not distinguish them from what they came from: refused service, charged double, run out of towns at dusk. Communities are routinely burned out by neighboring lords or witch-hunters, and they rebuild elsewhere — they teach their children against returning violence. Take patient work: scholars, archivists, healers, translators, the occasional witch. Avenor accepts them as full citizens; the Queen's College has trained Raknid witches for over a century, who serve the realm with exacting practice and fierce loyalty. Dress simply in muted earth-tones — undyed linens, soft leathers, layered scarves and travel-cloaks — sometimes ornamented with small objects woven from their own silk.
Raknid.quirk: Your movements are unnervingly silent and precise, causing others to notice you only when you intend them to.
abilities: Web-Sense

Primes.name: Primes
Primes.description: The elders of Hedoria — already a people before the Ensoulment, with histories that run further back than any other race's. Tall, heavy-built, bear-bodied and broad-shouldered, dense white fur thickening at chest, neck, and tail. Pale chimpanzee-like face with a long lion muzzle, long furry tail used for balance and gesture, dexterous five-fingered hands, broad padded snow-feet. Robe in deep blue, ochre, vermilion, and gold, layered and embroidered with clan, monastery, and lineage markings — small bone relics (finger-bones, teeth, jaw fragments bound in worked metal) worn at throat, chest, wrists, and belt. Live in great cliff-cut monasteries terraced into the base of cold mountains, hung with banners and skull-iconography, with bone-vaults cut deep into the rock. Seven great monasteries currently active; lost vaults from the Unmade era are recovered across generations. Practice ancestor-magic: a slow, exact, unflashy art of invoking specific named dead by rite and offering, drawing on chained lineages of ancestors back across millennia. Excellent at knowledge, healing, divination, true-naming, untangling curses, and long-time work — useless for battle-magic. Power is anchored: a Prime at home with full vault-access is enormously powerful; a Prime on the road, carrying only three or four portable relics (named ancestors in their own right), is a fraction of that. The wizard colleges fear them quietly — Primes hold the actual historical record the colleges have spent centuries rewriting. Black-market grave-robbing of Prime bones exists; the magic does not work for thieves, and the families involved suffer untraceable misfortunes across generations. Did not fight the first Wartide alongside Avenoria; the bone-vaults that made that decision still hold the magic, still counsel preservation, and still resist young Primes who want to commit to the second. Long-standing peerage with Avenor has gone quiet under Sephilia's reordering. The Primes will warn, document, and watch. Their monasteries will survive whatever comes.
Primes.quirk: You occasionally hear the soft, indistinct whispers of your ancestors when you are in dire need of guidance.
abilities: Ancestor-Call

Fernwarg.name: Fernwarg
Fernwarg.description: Wolf-and-fern people of the central forests. Tall, lean, upright, digitigrade, clawed hands and feet. Wolf-derived face — long muzzle, mobile ears, amber/yellow eyes, full canid expression-register. Coat is fur grown in fern-frond layers along back/shoulders/arms/tail, deep green to forest-dark, seasonally shifting; perfect camouflage in undergrowth. Spinal mane lifts in display, woven with bones, beads, and trophies — its size reads standing. Matriarchal by anatomy: females are larger, heavier, more muscular, with denser manes; pack-mothers carry headdresses of layered fronds. First birth is dangerous and the central passage of a female's life. Females choose mates. Males cannot lead their birth-pack — the most ambitious never will — so the ambitious leave and serve abroad as mercenaries, scouts, retainers. Packs are extended matrilines of 12–50, organized into forest-confederations. Share their forests with the Raknid by ancient unwritten compact (Fernwarg in open woodland, Raknid in deep old-growth). Two voices: long-carrying howls between packs, and an evening pack-laughter — a rising chittering chorus that disturbs human visitors. Locked in long political dispute with the bordering human kingdoms over forest rights; pressure building toward the third soul-war.
Fernwarg.quirk: Your coat subtly shifts color with the seasons, providing you with natural camouflage in forest environments.
abilities: Pack-Laughter Chorus

Threshi.name: Threshi
Threshi.description: Locust-mantis people, chitin-plated in dust-browns/tans/ochres/sand-yellows. Tall and narrow-bodied, triangular head with compound eyes and folding mandibles, segmented wing-panels along the back (short flights, long glides). Wartide race that held its ground when the working caught it. Strongly dimorphic: females are larger, broader, with chitin shovel-forearms (plowing, sowing, egg-laying — clutches of 20–40 several times yearly); males are leaner with folding sickle-blade forearms (harvest and war — same instrument). High mortality, faster replacement, surplus of unattached males every season. Aristocratic, ruled by warrior-king from a central capital; succession by challenge or claim, no king dies peacefully. Closed to outside trade. After every harvest, landless males ride out to take land, grain, and Quelled from the human borders — three centuries of inches has put significant former human territory under Threshi grain. Periodic field-worker risings, brutally suppressed.
Threshi.quirk: Your folding mandibles and compound eyes make it difficult for others to read your intentions during negotiations.
abilities: Chitinous Glide

Quelled.name: Quelled
Quelled.description: Youngest race, lifted from the domesticated livestock of pre-Wartide human farms. Several lineages: pig-quell (broad, snouted, bristled), cow-quell (tall, horned, long-faced), sheep-quell (small, fleece-haired, soft-faced), fowl-quell (lean, sharp, feather-down). Strongly animal in feature; not all stand fully upright. Crude tongues, no writing, no shared history beyond a few generations, no civilization. Bred, slaughtered, and worked as livestock and labor across the human kingdoms outside Avenor — the agricultural foundation of human society there. Avenor outlawed the trade two generations ago and grants escapees citizenship under the queen's writ; this is one of the chief reasons the rest of human Hedoria considers Avenor a traitor realm. Free Quelled elsewhere are isolated individuals — rare, hidden, short-lived.
Quelled.quirk: Your humble origins grant you an unassuming presence that causes others to often overlook you in a crowd.
abilities: Livestock Endurance

Vorok.name: Vorok
Vorok.description: Wartide-blooded miners, the continent's primary source of minerals, possess bodies that adapt to heavy labor over a lifetime. They operate under a complex system of house contracts and possess a supernatural ore-sense that makes them peerless prospectors.
Vorok.quirk: Your deep, grinding voice and casual use of obscene language make you stand out in formal social settings.
abilities: Ore-Sense

Human.name: Human
Human.description: Humans. Pre-Ensoulment majority race of Hedoria, dominant before the Wartide and reduced by it to four peripheral kingdoms — Avenor on the southern river-coast, Udorath on the western Whitestone, Nadorim in the northern lake-country, and Kelmar at the north end of the Shieldcap mountains. The interior of the continent is held by the lifted and wartide races. Still among the most numerous peoples on the continent. Shorter than Hofnar, Prime, or Draklid; taller than Molvar. Lifespan around seventy years. Coloring and build vary by region: pale and dark-haired in Udorath, ruddy and broad in Kelmar, fairer and lighter-built in Nadorim, mixed in Avenor. Dress varies by kingdom — Udorathi in well-cut linen and wool with chalk and salt-blue dyes; Kelmari in heavy practical garments for cold and the saddle, with house-colors at the shoulder and worked metal at belt and collar; Nadorim in plain tough cloth and oiled leathers against lake-damp, waterfowl-feather trim on formal wear; Avenorians in the restrained palette of the marble city.
Human.quirk: Your sheer tenacity often compels you to find solutions to problems that others have long since abandoned as hopeless.
abilities: Adaptive Learning

Soldier.name: Soldier
Soldier.description: A drilled fighter of one of the kingdom levies, household guards, town watches, or marches forces. Has stood in a line, held a position, and come out of an engagement at less than full strength more than once. The discipline carries.
Soldier.quirk: You read tactical ground without consciously meaning to.
abilities: Veteran

Mercenary.name: Mercenary
Mercenary.description: A free-company fighter taking contracts wherever the work is. Kit assembled across years of campaigns, loyalties priced by season, the season itself respected — a mercenary who breaks a contract while it runs is not a mercenary anyone hires next year.
Mercenary.quirk: You honor a signed contract through the season. Off-contract, you make your own choices.
abilities: Veteran
Hold the Contract

Hedge-Wizard.name: Hedge-Wizard
Hedge-Wizard.description: A man with the spark who slipped through, was trained privately, or learned what he could from a retired brother gone unsanctioned. Independent, unsanctioned, watched. The Brotherhood absorbs him where it can; where it cannot, it pays attention. He carries whatever staff he can get — a homemade one, an inherited piece, a smith-bought instrument, occasionally something stolen or scavenged; he upgrades when he can afford to or when fortune brings him a better one.
Hedge-Wizard.quirk: You watch the road behind you. The Brotherhood watches you back.
abilities: Standing Wall

Hedge-Witch.name: Hedge-Witch
Hedge-Witch.description: A village practitioner working in quiet violation of kingdom law (or openly in Avenor as a freelance outside the College). Helps with births, fevers, contraceptive draughts, locks, the small protective workings the local lord pretends not to notice. Modest reservoir filled where she can manage it — black-market potion, careful partner, rare beast-source. Lives in constant low-grade danger and has rehearsed the fire she will set in the cottage if the Brotherhood comes for her.
Hedge-Witch.quirk: You read every visitor for what they are, especially the polite ones.
abilities: Hedge-Working
Apothecary's Touch

Consort of the College.name: Consort of the College
Consort of the College.description: A male wizard of Avenor, contracted under the Queen's College donor-line system as a partner to one or more witches. The original cooperative tradition the Brotherhood broke from. Honored, lawful in Avenor, doctrinally unthinkable in the three kingdoms. A consort generates mana like any wizard and donates it to his contracted witches; senior consorts work cooperatively in the field, multiplying the witch's range past anything the Brotherhood can match alone.
Consort of the College.quirk: You think of mana as something to give. The Brotherhood has a different word for what you are.
abilities: Standing Wall
Cooperative Filling

Mana-Hunter.name: Mana-Hunter
Mana-Hunter.description: A specialist tracker and forager of magical-creature essence for the witch trade. The work runs across a moral range. The clean end is licensed: Avenorian houses keep stagling-meadows and managed sylvan groves and pay foresters to maintain the herds; lampfowl-egg gatherers and antler-foragers work the seasonal cycle. The ugly end is the captive trade — sylvans and glasshorns trapped and held for repeated milking — officially forbidden in most kingdoms, licensed in most kingdoms, ugly and known to be ugly. A given mana-hunter sits somewhere on that range, and most of them have stories from both ends.
Mana-Hunter.quirk: You see a creature first by the size of its essence.
abilities: Mana-Hunter Track
Brew Mana Potion

Prime Ancestor-Mage.name: Prime Ancestor-Mage
Prime Ancestor-Mage.description: A monastery-trained Prime working through the bone-line of named ancestors. White fur, lion-muzzled face, deep blue robes heavy with embroidery and ancestor-tokens. Carries three or four portable relics on the road — far less than vault-access at home, but the bones know their work. Excellent at knowledge, healing, divination, true-naming, untangling curses. Useless in a battle of rates.
Prime Ancestor-Mage.quirk: You consult the relic before you commit to anything that matters.
abilities: Ancestor-Call
Mend by Lineage
Bone-Reading

Prime Warden.name: Prime Warden
Prime Warden.description: A monastery-warrior of one of the seven great Prime monasteries — broad-shouldered and twelve feet of cold-country muscle, in heavy blue and crimson over layered fur, two-handed mace or polearm sized to the body. The monasteries do not project force outward. When the wardens come down from the mountains, something has gone seriously wrong.
Prime Warden.quirk: You speak rarely. When you arrive in a village, the village notices.
abilities: Veteran
Standing Wall

Hold-Warrior.name: Hold-Warrior
Hold-Warrior.description: A trained close-combat fighter of one of the great clan or noble households — Draklid clan-hold, Threshi noble-band, Vorok house-security, Kelmari marches-house, Hofnar clan-guard, Cephalen House-marine, Fernwarg pack-hunter. The kit and culture vary by house, the underlying discipline does not. Seasoned for the kind of fight the household runs.
Hold-Warrior.quirk: You read a hall first for who you would have to kill, and the appraisal does not surface as an expression.
abilities: Veteran

Hunter.name: Hunter
Hunter.description: A trapper, game-hunter, or back-country tracker working the kingdoms' forests, the steppe edges, the Plateau scree. Reads trail, season, and weather. Carries the trade home — pelts, meat, the occasional troublesome predator brought down for bounty. The same hands serve well in scout work, marches-cycle skirmish, and the work of clearing grist warrens and tarkle infestations from settled country.
Hunter.quirk: You see tracks the rest of the party walks past.
abilities: Marches-Reader

Crafter.name: Crafter
Crafter.description: A skilled hand of one of the trades — smith, carpenter, weaver, mason, cooper, leatherworker, instrument-maker, brewer, dyer, baker. The whole human and non-human world of made things runs through these hands. A crafter knows the materials, the tools, the workshop register, and the quiet politics of guild and license that govern the work in any given town.
Crafter.quirk: You assess any room first by what was made in it and how well.
abilities: Smith's Eye

Healer.name: Healer
Healer.description: A practitioner of the body's ordinary repair — apothecary, midwife, bonesetter, herbalist, surgeon, household physician. Not a witch, though many village healers are quietly both. Knows wounds, fevers, births, draughts, and the particular medicine each race needs that a stranger to the race would get wrong.
Healer.quirk: You triage a room without meaning to and remember faces by what they were dying of.
abilities: Apothecary's Touch

Drifter.name: Drifter
Drifter.description: An unrooted person of the towns and roads — traveling musician, gambler, off-season laborer, sex-worker, pickpocket, beggar, courier of small cargoes. Lives at the edges of settled life. Knows which inns turn you away, which constables are corruptible, which back roads run between kingdoms unwatched.
Drifter.quirk: You read a room first for who is paying attention to you and second for the exits.

Apothecary.name: Apothecary
Apothecary.description: A specialist of the alchemical trades — potions, reagents, philtres, the assessment of old magical items. The closest a non-magical person gets to a working life with magic. Brews healing draughts and contraceptives openly; brews mana potions where the kingdom permits and quietly where it doesn't; reads pre-Ensoulment items for the merchants and lords who bring them in. A senior apothecary in a large town commands prices that outpace most physicians.
Apothecary.quirk: You read every cargo first by what reagents it might be hiding.
abilities: Brew Healing Potion
Brew Reagent
Identify Old Item

Forbidden Working.name: Forbidden Working
Forbidden Working.description: The bearer practices magic outside the line — non-consenting essence-sources, hexcraft, compulsion of the will, the corner of the trade nobody admits to. Power and access at the cost of standing in any kingdom that finds out, and a personal weight the witch carries whether anyone finds out or not.
Forbidden Working.quirk: You have decided that the price is worth paying. You have not told anyone you trust.

School of Projection.name: School of Projection
School of Projection.description: The College's combat-magic specialty. The witch's projective working — flame, cold, light, force at distance — is faster to bring up and cheaper per use than for a generalist. The school's masters are the witches the Brotherhood fears most in open engagement.
School of Projection.quirk: You see a fight first as a question of geometry — where the working will land, what it will catch.
abilities: Heat Lance
Witchfire
Cold Wall

School of the Hidden.name: School of the Hidden
School of the Hidden.description: The College's glamour and concealment specialty. The witch's working slides past the eye, the appearance of a stranger holds for hours, the patrol passes within reach. The school favored by witches who travel quietly outside Avenor and by the queen's couriers.
School of the Hidden.quirk: You walk a market and notice nobody noticing you.
abilities: Veil
Misdirection

School of Sight.name: School of Sight
School of Sight.description: The College's scrying and intent-reading specialty. The witch reads the working in a room, the truth in a mouth, the line of someone watched at distance. The school behind every Avenorian intelligence operation worth running.
School of Sight.quirk: You read a room before you've fully entered it, and the read is rarely wrong.
abilities: Distance-Sight
Read Intent
Trace Working

School of the Body.name: School of the Body
School of the Body.description: The College's healing and birth specialty. The witch closes the wound, draws the fever, eases the labor, sets the bone with the working doing the slow patient work the body alone could not. The continent's quietest specialty and its most consistently useful — every village has needed this craft, lawful or not.
School of the Body.quirk: You triage rooms without meaning to and remember faces by what they were dying of.
abilities: Mend Body
Ease the Birth

School of the Hand.name: School of the Hand
School of the Hand.description: The College's telekinetic specialty. The witch moves weight at distance, hurls weapons, holds doors, drags fallen comrades clear. Less showy than projection, more useful in a tight room. The school senior consorts pair best with — co-working a hand-spell triples its weight without tripling the reservoir cost.
School of the Hand.quirk: You catalogue a room first by what is loose and what is fixed.
abilities: Force Pull
Hold the Door
Hurl

Marches Drilled.name: Marches Drilled
Marches Drilled.description: Trained on the Kelmari winter line. The Threshi raid cycle has been the bearer's school for years — read the ground, place the lance, ride down what does not break and disengage from what does. The most experienced field discipline in human Hedoria.
Marches Drilled.quirk: You read the season into every meadow and judge it for the Threshi line you would have ridden across it last year.
abilities: Marches Charge
Marches-Reader

Marsh Skirmisher.name: Marsh Skirmisher
Marsh Skirmisher.description: Trained on the Udorathi fen-edge. Strike from cover, fall back through ground a stranger can't read, ambush the heavy column that thought it had crossed open country. The discipline that has held the western frontier in worse moments than the present one.
Marsh Skirmisher.quirk: You read a marsh first for the line of footing only the locals see.
abilities: Marsh Ambush

Lake Archer.name: Lake Archer
Lake Archer.description: Trained at the Nadorim seasonal musters. Loose long arrows from boat or pier-head, read wind across water, place a volley on a column that has no cover. The kingdom's principal military art and a quietly devout discipline; bowmen touch the Hand-sigil before each volley.
Lake Archer.quirk: You read wind off water automatically, and notice every arc your peers haven't accounted for.
abilities: Lake-Volley

Free-Company Veteran.name: Free-Company Veteran
Free-Company Veteran.description: The bearer has run a contract season as a captain or senior of a small mixed-race mercenary band. Tactics, contract-language, the politics of holding a company together across a winter — the experience reads in every move.
Free-Company Veteran.quirk: You read a band of strangers first for what they cost to feed.
abilities: Free-Company Captain

Duelist Trained.name: Duelist Trained
Duelist Trained.description: The single-combat specialist. Trained in a school of a kingdom or on the Draklid challenge-form, the bearer reads an opponent in beats ahead. Useful in noble duels, the Draklid challenge, the back-alley fight that won't be stopped.
Duelist Trained.quirk: You watch a fight beginning to read who finishes it.
abilities: Duelist's Eye

Pair-Bonded Brother.name: Pair-Bonded Brother
Pair-Bonded Brother.description: The brother has formed a pair-bond with another from his agoge cohort — comrades across decades, deployed together by mutual choice. The order does not police these bonds. The pair reads each other's openings without speech.
Pair-Bonded Brother.quirk: You think of yourself as half of a pair. The bond was the choice that defined you.
abilities: Pair-Bonded

Hollow-Touched.name: Hollow-Touched
Hollow-Touched.description: The bearer has, at some point in their life, drawn power from a Wartide-fragment source — a Hollow walked, a demonic remnant overcome, an old corrupted battlefield held too long. Power follows. The bearer is changed by it in ways nobody catalogues cleanly.
Hollow-Touched.quirk: You feel the Hollows from miles away. They feel you back.
abilities: Hollow-Touched Draw

Pinnacle of the Reservoir.name: Pinnacle of the Reservoir
Pinnacle of the Reservoir.description: Born under a star the College reads the same way every generation — the witch whose reservoir holds far more than any of her cohort, whose ceiling approaches the historical record. The body pays for it; the witch is physically slighter and tires faster, and her workings cost her in ways other witches' do not. At a full reservoir she is the most dangerous person in any human kingdom, and the College knows where she lives.
Pinnacle of the Reservoir.quirk: Other witches feel your reservoir from across a room. The Brotherhood marks your name when it hears it.

Pinnacle of Mana.name: Pinnacle of Mana
Pinnacle of Mana.description: The wizard whose body produces mana faster and at greater capacity than any of his agoge-cohort. The Brotherhood marks him early, deploys him often, and is at once proud of him and quietly worried about him. Some senior masters note that the men with the strongest pools historically have the hardest time staying loyal to the doctrine.
Pinnacle of Mana.quirk: Other wizards read the strength of your output and adjust their tone accordingly.

Disowned.name: Disowned
Disowned.description: The bearer was cut from a clan, an order, or a house — Draklid stripped of clan-sigil, Brotherhood man broken from the order, Vorok blacklisted across the inter-house compacts, Hofnar driven from a Rideing for a contract broken. The bearer takes work no member-in-good-standing would touch and is more dangerous for it. The reason is not asked.
Disowned.quirk: You do not introduce yourself by lineage. The lineage is the topic you change.

Refugee under the Writ.name: Refugee under the Writ
Refugee under the Writ.description: The bearer reached Avenor as a refugee — Quell escapee, Threshi dissident, Raknid burned-out community survivor — and was received under the queen's writ. They carry the small civic token of citizenship and a long quiet story of how they got here. Welcome in Avenor; visible everywhere else.
Refugee under the Writ.quirk: You hold the writ-token close in any kingdom that does not honor it, which is three out of four.

Wagon-Born.name: Wagon-Born
Wagon-Born.description: Raised in a Hofnar clan-wagon on the long routes. The bearer knows roads, currencies, customs, and the quiet politics of every market between the southern and northern grasslands. Speaks four trade-tongues at a working level by fifteen.
Wagon-Born.quirk: You name each town first by the road that gets you out of it.
abilities: Trade-Tongue Negotiator

Cephalen-Schooled.name: Cephalen-Schooled
Cephalen-Schooled.description: The bearer trained in a Cephalen House for a season or more — color-discipline, the codes of obligation, the formal courtesy that masks negotiation. Rare for a non-Cephalen; the Houses do not teach outsiders lightly. Useful in any negotiation that has consequences.
Cephalen-Schooled.quirk: You hold an expression for as long as you choose, and choose deliberately.

Wizard.name: Wizard
Wizard.description: A male human with the spark — the rare biological capacity to generate mana from his own body. Training comes through the Brotherhood at Telinor (the formal path: agoge from boyhood, a decade of magical and martial drill, lifelong celibate doctrine, the dark uniform of the order) or through informal channels (hedge-tutoring, retired brothers gone unsanctioned, private masters paid in coin). Either way he works the four domains of staff-work — striking, bracing, warding, motion — and progresses through staves over his career: a training staff first, then field-issue, eventually a master's staff if rank or fortune carries him there. The Brotherhood claims most of the field and watches the rest.
Wizard.quirk: You think with the staff in hand — the small pool of mana measured against the working, the body's weight committed behind each strike. Without the staff you feel half-armed.
abilities: Standing Wall
Wind-Slow
Hardened Strike

Witch.name: Witch
Witch.description: A trained witch — typically a human woman, occasionally a Draklid, Cephalen, Raknid, or freed Quell. Her power lives in a reservoir she fills with male essence and spends on workings: projection, glamour, sight, body, hand. Training comes through the Queen's College in Avenor (open practice, lawful, the consort-line system keeping her supplied at home) or through the hedge tradition (informal teaching, careful filling on what she can find, the practice concealed from neighbors). The five working domains are the same; only the access to consort-line resources and the legal protection differ. Outside Avenor every witch lives with the threat of arrest, exile, or quiet death — even the College-trained, when they cross borders.
Witch.quirk: You think in mana — what each working will cost, when you can fill, who in the room is the spark and who is not.
abilities: Hedge-Working
Witchlight
Conjure Cold
Cooperative Filling

Courtesan.name: Courtesan
Courtesan.description: A trained practitioner of paid intimacy in courtesan-house, tavern, or street apprenticeship. You read people as a craft, manage risk with precision, and know every layer of the trade. The work grants both coin and unmatched skill in reading desire and negotiating its terms.
Courtesan.quirk: You read a room first for who is paying attention and second for the terms of what they want.

Quelled-Marked.name: Quelled-Marked
Quelled-Marked.description: You survived the Quelled-trade, the structural sexual slavery that touches every beast-market and breeding-house across Hedoria. The mark on your body reads what happened. The skill it taught is undeniable, and the hardness it left behind keeps you standing when others would break.
Quelled-Marked.quirk: You have seen what others cannot imagine. The things you survived made you unbreakable in ways quiet people do not speak of.
abilities: Hardened to It

Drawing Witch.name: Drawing Witch
Drawing Witch.description: You are a witch who chose the direct path over the hedge craft. You draw mana from partners through sexual intimacy, uncut and efficient. This is the taboo technique most witches refuse, the one that marks you dangerous in the eyes of every kingdom that learns what you are. The Brotherhood hunts you before they hunt others.
Drawing Witch.quirk: You walk into rooms and the air changes. People feel drawn to you before they know why, and when they learn what draws them, they recoil.
abilities: Wild Draw

Trader.name: Trader
Trader.description: A merchant who buys low and sells high across kingdom borders and trade-routes. Reads ledgers, factor-letters, and contract clauses with ease; knows guild licenses and the customs of every major market town. Could be trained on a Hofnar wagon, in a Cephalen counting-house, as a human burgher-merchant, or through years of freelance broker work.
Trader.quirk: You see a room first by what it is willing to trade.
abilities: Read the Market

Diplomat.name: Diplomat
Diplomat.description: A court envoy or treaty-bearer trained to navigate the delicate spaces between hostile powers. Reads rooms with precision, drafts careful language, and carries word between parties who will not speak directly. Could be a human kingdom-emissary, Cephalen House ambassador, Threshi border-envoy, or independent broker working freelance contracts.
Diplomat.quirk: You say what each side needs to hear, framed the way they are ready to receive it.
abilities: Bridge the Gap

Spy.name: Spy
Spy.description: An informant, watcher, or intelligence-handler trained to listen, move unseen, and carry word through dangerous territory. Skilled at manufacturing false tells and reading the social signatures of intrigue. Could be Cephalen-trained in color-discipline, a hedge-network informant, a kingdom agent, or freelance work.
Spy.quirk: You listen to what people don't say, and the silence tells you everything.
abilities: Read the Room

Pit-Foreman.name: Pit-Foreman
Pit-Foreman.description: A mining specialist who reads ground-water, gas pockets, fault-lines, ore-body structure, ventilation, and tunnel-shoring with practiced eye. Oversees extraction and safety in difficult ground. Could be a Vorok consortium foreman, a kingdom's quarry-master, or a foreign engineer trained in Vorok mines on contract.
Pit-Foreman.quirk: You assess ground first by what it will give up and what it will take from you.
abilities: Ground-Sense

Sailor.name: Sailor
Sailor.description: A deck-hand, oarsman, or ship-fighter with salt-weathered hands and the muscle to work rope, sail, and boarding pike through a long voyage. Reads weather, current, and the small language of the ship with instinctive ease. Could be Cephalen crew, Nadorim coastal hand, lake-fisher of the Avenor or Kelmar lakes, or river-pilot of the great waterways.
Sailor.quirk: You feel the water's mood before the crew sees it in the sky.
abilities: Weather-Sense

Pilot.name: Pilot
