Pilot.description: A master of currents, charts, and channels who brings ships through treacherous harbor approaches, river bars, and dangerous straits. The difference between a safe passage and a wreck often rests on the pilot's read of invisible water. Could be a Cephalen harbor-pilot, Nadorim river-master, kingdom navigator, or independent contracted specialist.
Pilot.quirk: You read water before you read the sky, and your crew trusts what you say.
abilities: Channel-Read

Scribe.name: Scribe
Scribe.description: A literate clerk, copyist, or court-recorder skilled in drafting contracts, copying texts, and maintaining ledgers with exacting care. The work grants authority in any kingdom that values the written word. Could be a kingdom bureaucrat, Threshi court specialist, Hofnar contract-drafter, Brotherhood archivist, or freelance letter-writer.
Scribe.quirk: You read what was written and hear what was not said.

Watch-Officer.name: Watch-Officer
Watch-Officer.description: A town watch sergeant, kingdom investigator, or harbor-warden carrying arrest authority within jurisdiction. Trained to read crime scenes, run hard questions, and manage the violence that comes with law. Could be Avenorian watch, Udorath garrison-officer, or Hofnar caravan-warden.
Watch-Officer.quirk: You read guilt before a person opens their mouth.
abilities: Interrogate

Outlaw.name: Outlaw
Outlaw.description: An exile, brigand, or fugitive living back-country beyond the reach of the law. Learned to read pursuit, know the fence-network, and move unseen through territories where being found means death. Could be a banished hedge-witch, a Vorok blacklisted from the houses, a Threshi who broke contract, or a deserter from any kingdom.
Outlaw.quirk: You read authority the moment it enters a room, and you know exactly where the door is.
abilities: Vanish

Caravan Guard.name: Caravan Guard
Caravan Guard.description: A hired wagon-protector with years of cross-border journey-fighting in the blood. Practiced at reading ambush ground, the pace of a long road, and how to hold a circled camp against raiders. Could be a Vorok exile, free-company hand, kingdom veteran on contract, or any drifter who took the work.
Caravan Guard.quirk: You assess every piece of ground first for who has the advantage.
abilities: Camp-Hold

Quick-Witted.name: Quick-Witted
Quick-Witted.description: A mind that moves fast — catching tells before others register the question, parsing situations with rapid clarity. Useful in negotiation, combat, and any moment where the first read wins.
Quick-Witted.quirk: You see the angle before it is played.

Iron Stomach.name: Iron Stomach
Iron Stomach.description: A digestive system that survives bad food, dubious water, and mild poisons without flinching. The bearer eats what others leave behind and walks away better for it.
Iron Stomach.quirk: You pick at suspect food without hesitation and never sicken from it.

Sharp-Eyed.name: Sharp-Eyed
Sharp-Eyed.description: Distance and low-light vision that catches what is faint, far, or hidden in shadow. Useful in any environment where others strain to see.
Sharp-Eyed.quirk: You spot what others miss in fog, dusk, or the gloom of a distant ridgeline.

Light-Footed.name: Light-Footed
Light-Footed.description: Stealth and balance that allow silent movement on any ground and recovery from a slip without crashing. The bearer is never the one whose step gives the position away.
Light-Footed.quirk: You move quiet on any surface and catch yourself before you fall.

Silver-Tongued.name: Silver-Tongued
Silver-Tongued.description: A gift for reading what people want to hear and giving a version of it. The bearer talks their way through doors that others pound against.
Silver-Tongued.quirk: You choose the angle the room is already leaning toward and lean harder.

Apprenticed Under a Master.name: Apprenticed Under a Master
Apprenticed Under a Master.description: The bearer learned a trade from someone who really knew it — the depth of knowledge that comes only from years at the bench or forge with a skilled hand teaching the subtleties. Hold high standards, see slipshod work for what it is.
Apprenticed Under a Master.quirk: You know how it should be done, and sloppy work offends you on a fundamental level.

Survived a Wasting.name: Survived a Wasting
Survived a Wasting.description: Long illness, plague, or fever that left scars on the body and hardness in the spirit. The bearer is tougher for what the sickness took and what they had to do to walk out of it.
Survived a Wasting.quirk: You read how a body is failing because you know all the ways a body can fail.

Lost to Captivity.name: Lost to Captivity
Lost to Captivity.description: Held prisoner for a year or more — mine, raid-camp, debt-prison, breeding compound, or lord's dungeon. Came out hardened in ways that do not heal. The bearer reads coercion fast and knows when someone is being squeezed.
Lost to Captivity.quirk: You see the cage in any room, even rooms made of nothing but air.

Crossed the Unforgiving.name: Crossed the Unforgiving
Crossed the Unforgiving.description: Walked alone across a hostile region — the Black Steppe, Hollow-fringe, Threshi border-zone, or deep desert — and came out the other side changed. Know how far rest can carry, when to push, and when to camp.
Crossed the Unforgiving.quirk: You assess the land first by what it will cost to cross it.

Late Arrival.name: Late Arrival
Late Arrival.description: The bearer came to their present trade late, after a different life already lived. Brings cross-domain perspective that younger trainees lack. See what specialists miss because they only know one way.
Late Arrival.quirk: You remember how it was done before, and that memory is useful more often than you would think.

Recruit.name: Recruit
Recruit.description: One season of drill and at most one engagement under your belt. You can hold a line if pointed at it, follow orders, and look like a soldier from a distance — but the small skills that mark a veteran are still ahead of you. The discipline is starting to stick; the instinct is not yet there.
Recruit.quirk: You watch how the older soldiers stand at rest, and you copy it before you understand it.

Free-Hand.name: Free-Hand
Free-Hand.description: An independent for-hire taking whatever short work comes. No Free-Company badge yet, no name in the contract-circles, but you have a weapon, the willingness to use it, and the start of a reputation. The big road-contracts are not yet open to you; the small ones build the name that opens them.
Free-Hand.quirk: You quote your rate before they ask, because that's what you watched the veterans do.

Wizard Aspirant.name: Wizard Aspirant
Wizard Aspirant.description: A youth with the spark, in early years of formal training or under an unsanctioned tutor. Can hold a staff and lay simple workings — small strikes, light bracing — but the mana pool is small and the four disciplines have not yet settled into instinct. Years of drill still ahead before the practice is real.
Wizard Aspirant.quirk: You count the mana you can hold against the working you want to do, and the working keeps winning.

Witch Initiate.name: Witch Initiate
Witch Initiate.description: A novice in early training — College first-year, hedge-tutored from her teens, or just past the change that revealed the gift. Can hold a small reservoir and do trivial workings; nothing combat-grade yet. The first year is mostly nervy practice and learning when not to.
Witch Initiate.quirk: You spend half a working watching to see if it took, and the other half worrying it didn't.

Wagon-Hand.name: Wagon-Hand
Wagon-Hand.description: Junior on a Hofnar wagon, kingdom road-crew, or independent caravan. Strong back, fresh eyes, learning the routes and the contract patterns from the seniors. No agency in trade decisions yet; you do what you're told, watch what you're shown, and earn your standing through the first season.
Wagon-Hand.quirk: You memorize the order of stops on a route the way other people memorize songs.

Runner.name: Runner
Runner.description: A dispatch-rider, kingdom messenger, or scout-runner — the youth they send when the road is dangerous and the message has to arrive. Travels light, fast, and across country others find difficult. Not yet trusted with the larger work of long contracts or deep scouting, but the routes that build that trust are exactly what you are running now.
Runner.quirk: You judge a road by how fast you can leave it, not by how comfortable it is to walk.

Auroch.text: Auroch: Wild unensouled cattle of the Deep Steppes and Long Steppe—massive bovines with bull-shoulders and horn-spreads the width of a wagon. Solitary bulls range the open grass; small breeding groups of cows and calves graze at distance. The Hofnar do not eat them, holding to the old kinship with their lifted cousins, but every other people will. A charging auroch goes through a cart and the man behind the cart, and herds spook into stampedes that level whatever they cross.

Tuskboar.text: Tuskboar: Foul-tempered boar of the Reach margins, Whitestone fens, and Avenor backwoods. Carries plague—the meat must be cured with care or it kills the eater. Solitary or sow-with-young, charges anything that crosses its path. Tusks split a leg at the thigh; the small eyes are red and constantly weeping. Farmers around the Reach margins lose more children to tuskboars than to anything else in the wood.

Crag-cat.text: Crag-cat: Lynx of the Shieldcaps, Hinderance Highlands, and Impasse heights. Pale-coated, ear-tufted, the size of a large dog at the shoulder. Solitary daylight hunter—takes goats and the occasional shepherd. Stalks across the scree on padded feet that leave no track and commits in a single leap from above. A crag-cat does not stalk what it cannot kill, and the leap is rarely wasted.

Rhok.text: Rhok: Flightless ground-bird of the Long Steppe and the Sun-Scoured canyon floors—ostrich-built, tall as a man, with naked legs and a heavy plumed body. Kicks with a single splitting claw on each foot, the kick strong enough to disembowel a horse. Runs faster than a horse over distance and longer than a horse before it tires. Solitary or in mated pairs. Aggressive in the rutting season and indifferent the rest. Rhok plumes are worn at every festival in three kingdoms.

Wisent.text: Wisent: Forest bison of the Reach river-valleys and meadow-corridors—shaggy, heavy-shouldered, dark-coated. Solitary bulls and small breeding groups graze the open stretches between stands of old-growth. A wisent that has not been disturbed will let a traveler pass at distance, but the distance is well-known to anyone who has lived through being told it. Closer than that and the bull charges, and a wisent charge does not stop until something stops it.

Throatworm.text: Throatworm: Knee-thick burrower of the loam—four to six feet long when fully extended, segmented in dull chitin and ringed with hooked feeding-mouths. Erupts from beneath at close range and fights through rather than retreating. Mound-and-disturbance signs mark colony territory in the Reach margins, the Long Steppe edges, and Avenor pasture country. A grazing herd that goes still and looks at the ground has felt one moving below.

Husk.text: Husk: Once human. Survivors of Wartide attacks where something drained them and let the body walk—usually the work of a Bloodthane, sometimes a Succor, sometimes older things in the deeper Hollows. Pale, bent, milky-eyed, mouths working without words. What was a person is gone. Reduced to primal instincts: hunger, fear, and the urge to return to a place that mattered. A husk drifts back toward the home it lived in for years, sometimes finding it, sometimes finding what replaced it. Bounty paid in most kingdoms. The bounty is the only mercy left to give them.

Gristlemaw.text: Gristlemaw: Knee-high scaly thing, dull grey, heavily built around an oversized hinged jaw that takes up most of the head. Lurks in barrows, farmstead cellars, and the underspaces of old buildings. Takes one or two big meals a season—a sheep, a watchdog, a child gone too far in the dark—and rests between. Found alone or with a single mate. Slow, but the jaw closes faster than anyone expects, and a gristlemaw that has bitten a kneecap will brace its weight and drag.

Pinker.text: Pinker: Hairless pale primate the size of a large dog—naked-skinned, knuckle-walking, climbs walls. One or two haunt a ruined building, a back-alley cellar, or the empty floor above a tavern's storage. Pries with long fingers, breaks shutters, takes infants and small livestock. Patient enough to scout a household for days before striking and clever enough to use simple tools—a pried board, a stolen knife, a weighted rope. The eyes are too forward-set for an animal and too cold for anything else.

Drakehound.text: Drakehound: Wartide-bred war-dog. Waist-high at the shoulder, scaled along the spine and flanks, long-jawed, bred to run prey to exhaustion across open country. Mute pack-hunter—three to five, no baying. Found across the back-country of every region where the broken hosts left them and they bred wild. The pack does not bark, does not howl, does not bay. The first sign is the silence that comes when the wood-noises stop.

Cliff-wyrm.text: Cliff-wyrm: Limbless serpent of the Sun-Scoured Plateau and the upper ranges of the Shieldcaps. Length of three wagons, thick as a man through the body, scales the color of the rock it suns itself on. Suns on flat stone in late afternoon, drops on prey from a cleft, constricts, swallows. A cliff-wyrm that has eaten will not eat again for weeks, but a cliff-wyrm that has not eaten will follow a caravan for days waiting for a straggler.

Mawpig.text: Mawpig: Wartide-bred carrion-beast. Squat tusked thing the size of a calf, mouth that splits the skull horizontally on a hinged lower jaw. Eats anything organic—the dead of any side, the dying, the fallen, the freshly buried. Solo or paired across the wild back-country of every region. Charges, doesn't ambush. The name is the right one. The mouth is most of what it is.

Striglaw.text: Striglaw: Tall thin biped, twin pairs of arms—upper pair gripping, lower pair barbed. Wartide-bred terror-troops of the lost campaigns, now solitary wanderers in the Reach back-country, the Impasse, and the Hollows fringes. Walks the woodland in plain view and picks fights with anything it crosses. The four-armed silhouette is the warning. By the time a traveler is close enough to count the arms, the traveler is too close.

Ulk.text: Ulk: Slime, dog-sized to bull-sized depending on age, semi-firm to the touch like very soft rubber, semi-transparent amber. Cuts under a blade rather than parts around it—the body holds shape but yields to edge. Ulk reproduce by hosting their seed-pearls inside larger animals, who pass them later; the engulfing is the first stage, and ulk will engulf prey of any gender to implant. Found in old galleries, ruin basements, Echoing Hollow, and the abandoned Whitestone mines. Slow but committed in pursuit, and an ulk that has scented a host does not lose the scent.

Slogger.text: Slogger: Aggressive slug the size of a calf—slate-grey, glistening, slow but committed. Marsh edges of the Whitestone, Lake Wildering shallows, and Reach bog-pools. Acid mucus strips contact—a hand laid on a slogger comes back to the bone. The blunt charge is what kills, the weight of the body breaking what it rolls over. The wide silver track through reed and mud marks where one has gone, and the track does not lie.

Brood.text: Brood: Cart-sized spider-thing, abdomen heavy with clutch, eight legs, six clustered eyes that catch lantern-light wrong. Lairs in deep Reach old-growth, the cave systems of the Shieldcap foothills and the Impasse, abandoned galleries, and Hollows fringes. The bite paralyzes; the ovipositor implants regardless of host's gender, into mouth, throat, or below. Hosts mostly survive. The Brood itself is a real fight—heavy chitin from above, abdomen vulnerable from below. Larders contain living people who can be freed if the Brood falls before they do.

Hookwight.text: Hookwight: Long pallid serpent the length of two men, jaw hinged to swallow what its head can clear. Grassland and meadow country across the continent—Long Steppe, Reach river-meadows, Avenor pasture-edges. Strikes from cover but commits and fights through rather than retreating. Venom causes a deep slackness in the muscle, a numbness that walks the body part by part. A man bitten and standing has minutes before he is a man bitten and lying down.

Beetlebull.text: Beetlebull: Ox-sized horned beetle of the Reach margins, Long Steppe edges, and Sun-Scoured canyon floors. Solitary or paired. Plates the back against pursuit—the carapace will turn an arrow at oblique angle—and charges with a single great horn that lifts what it strikes. Slow to turn. Fast in a straight line. A beetlebull on the charge sounds like a small wagon coming through the brush.

Mauler.text: Mauler: Ogre. Twelve feet tall, slab-shouldered, club-armed, lone keeper of a stretch of road or a pass. Builds itself a rough den from logs and dragged stone and leaves bones outside as a marker. Found on overland routes across the Reach, the Hinderance, and the Impasse. Slow, but cunning enough to set a simple ambush of fallen logs and dropped stones. Takes captives back to the den for use over days. A mauler does not eat its captives quickly, and a captive who fights well lives longer.

Bridge-troll.text: Bridge-troll: Squat broad troll of river-crossings and old culverts—Lake Wildering tributaries, Avenor backcountry, Reach river-valleys. Lairs under stone bridges, in collapsed culverts, in the deep weed-shadow at the foot of an old fording-stone. Speaks basic common-tongue, enough to name a price and curse a refusal. Demands toll in food, livestock, or person. Will let payers pass. Will not let non-payers pass. Skin grows back from wounds slowly but not from fire.

Skarn.text: Skarn: High-country troll of the Shieldcap upper passes and the Sun-Scoured plateau heights. Stone-skinned, two heads, the second mostly silent. Hunts goats and travelers. Lairs in glacier hollows and slot-canyon overhangs where the wind carries the smell of its kills away. Slow on level ground and terrifying on broken ground—the stone-skin is heavy and the legs are short, but on the cliff-faces a skarn climbs like the cliff itself has decided to come down on you.

Skellach.text: Skellach: Oxen-legged horned humanoid, tall as a man, knot-bearded, walks woodland clearings and ruin courtyards in the Reach and Avenor backwoods. Hunts alone or in pairs. Eats fruit and keeps an orchard-camp—a stretch of woodland tended in its own slow way, fruit trees encouraged, brambles cleared, bound captives kept for the games it likes to play. Speaks well, with a slow rural cadence, and enjoys conversation when not provoked. Fights with a heavy wood stave, longer than a man, used two-handed in a wide-arc style. Takes captives of either sex back to the orchard and binds them with woven vine—a particular fetish for the binding itself, and for letting other creatures of the wood take their turn with the bound. Releases them after, into the forest, dazed but living. Will sometimes accept a basket of good fruit and a length of cord in trade for not pressing the matter further.

Imp.text: Imp: Knee-high demonic remnant. Red or ash-grey skin, vestigial wings that don't carry weight, prehensile tail, sharp teeth. Solo encounters in ruins, cellars, and Hollows-fringe back-rooms. Small fire-magic—a thrown handful of flame, a burning palm-print on flesh—and a cunning mean enough to be a real fight in a closed room. Talkative, and most imps will negotiate before committing to violence. Their offers are clever, their terms unfavorable, and the small print binds.

Succor.text: Succor: Female-form demonic remnant, beautiful in a wrong way the mind smooths over until too late. Walks the road in plain sight near Hollows-fringes and old battlefields. Speaks with a warmth that is its first weapon. Charms by voice and touch, drains the charmed over a single night, leaves a Husk. Will fight directly when the charm fails—claws extend, the mouth opens wider than it should. A traveler who refuses the charm cleanly can sometimes parley; a succor that has been refused twice in the same season can be bargained with at unusually fair terms, by her standards.

Goret.text: Goret: Imp's larger cousin. Waist-high, thickset, broken horns, drools coal-smoke. Carries a crude iron weapon and uses it competently. Speaks in a low rasping common-tongue, swears constantly, prefers haggling to fighting when the haggling pays better. Dens in pairs in old foundries, abandoned galleries, and Hollows-fringe ruins. Will fight to the death over a hoard.

Glimmercat.text: Glimmercat: House-cat-sized, opalescent fur, eyes that hold a slow inner light. Solitary in open country across the continent—meadows, hedgerows, ruin-margins, the edges of cultivated land. Skittish but curious. A glimmercat will follow a traveler at distance for an afternoon, sit at the edge of a campfire, watch the night through, and be gone before dawn. Mana-touched in the small way that minor magical beasts are. Hedge-witches keep a stocked larder of glimmercat parts; the witch-academies do not bother.

Lumefox.text: Lumefox: Red-fox-sized, coat shifting between russet and silver depending on the angle of light, brush-tail tipped white. Solitary in meadows, hedgerows, and ruin-margins across the continent. Common enough to grind, valuable enough to bother—the bulk of the licensed Avenorian witch-trade is lumefox. Trapping is a profession in three kingdoms. A lumefox is intelligent in the quiet watching way of magical beasts and learns the lay of a trap-line within a season. The honest trappers replace their lines twice a year. The dishonest ones lose their hands.

Lampfowl.text: Lampfowl: Pheasant-sized ground bird of the Reach edges and the Whitestone uplands. Tail feathers glow soft yellow at dusk, brightest in the hour after sundown. Flightless, runs and fights when cornered—the spurred legs are heavy and the beak is hooked. A lampfowl roost is a faint glow under the brush at twilight. The tail feathers and eggs are valued reagents in alchemy: feathers for minor scrolls and light-charms, eggs as a base for hedge-tier potions and lesser working-charms. The material does not fill a witch on its own — only essence does — but lampfowl reagents take a working better than ordinary material and stretch a thin alchemy further.

Sylvan.text: Sylvan: Male humanoid magical creature, full-grown the size of a slight man, skin pearl or moss-green, found alone near old wells, deep pools, and sacred springs in the Reach and Avenor foothills. Beautiful in a still alien way, mute but watchful, fights with a small bone knife and minor water-magic. Mana-rich in the way a man is mana-rich, and far more so. Captured alive sells for many times the kill-price; the witch-academies want them for filling work, and a sylvan in good condition can fill a senior witch in a single session—the highest-quality donor source short of a Glasshorn. Officially forbidden in most kingdoms. Licensed in most kingdoms. The trade is ugly and most know it.

Naia.text: Naia: Female counterpart to the sylvan, found in the same waters and groves. Beautiful, mute, fights with a bone knife and minor water-magic. Naia parts do not fill a witch — material never does — but they are a high-value alchemical reagent that potentiates almost any working brewed around them, and an apothecary with naia stock can produce mana potions of better grade and higher yield from the same essence-source. The kill-trade hunts them for parts; the living-trade does not bother. The naia's only protection from the kill-trade is that the witch who needs naia parts is rarely the witch who has the men to find them.

Stagling.text: Stagling: Fawn-sized horned creature of the Reach meadow-corridors. Antlers carry a visible mana-glow at first light, brightest in the hour before sunrise. Runs but does not flee far. A common starter quarry for mana-hunters in their first season. Antlers grow back annually if the creature is spared, and the higher-grade trade is a managed harvest—Avenorian licensed houses keep stagling-meadows in the Reach foothills and pay foresters to keep the herds.

Pyrelynx.text: Pyrelynx: Cat the size of a wolf, coat the color of banked coals, warm to the touch even in winter. Sun-Scoured Plateau and southern Shieldcap foothills. Solitary, ambushes once and fights through. The warm-touch coat is the early-warning—a pyrelynx that has been near a place leaves the stone warm for an hour, and a hunter who knows the sign can tell when one has just left a kill.

Maulerbear.text: Maulerbear: Wartide-bred bear, twice the size of a true bear, shaggy and scarred, jaw plated with bony ridges. Solitary, claims a stretch of forest or broken country and walks it openly. Reach interior, Shieldcap foothills, Hinderance back-country. Will pursue across territories—the maulerbear that has decided you are its kill follows you home. Speaks little, but speaks—a slow rumbling common-tongue, used mostly to taunt and to name what it intends to do.

Bloodthane.text: Bloodthane: Wartide-officer remnant. Tall, chitin-plated, intelligent, command-class. Lairs in fortified ruins on the Hollows fringes—old keeps, ravine-fortresses, ruined towers—and walks the parapet of its claimed ground in the manner of the lord it once was. Drains humans to Husks by long touch, the touch lasting hours, the drained walking out at the end. Speaks fluently in any tongue it has heard, holds court in its ruin, and will receive emissaries who arrive correctly. Most emissaries do not arrive correctly. Commands lesser Wartide things in its territory—drakehound packs, husks, occasionally a Brood that has settled within reach.

Kelker.text: Kelker: Twelve feet tall, two stilt-legs, crown of arms around a central feeding-orifice. Strides across broken country at impossible speed—the stilt-legs cover ground a horse cannot. Sun-Scoured Plateau, the Impasse, Hollows fringes. Speaks rarely, in a voice that comes from the feeding-orifice and is not pleasant to hear. What it says is usually a question, and the questions are old—older than any human kingdom, older than the Wartide that bred it, old in a way that suggests the kelker is asking on behalf of something that has not yet arrived.

Loam-Drinker.text: Loam-Drinker: Vast burrowing worm of the deep tunnels and the worked-out homeland country. Surfaces in old pit-works to feed—the ground heaves, the head clears the soil, the segmented body keeps coming and coming. Speaks through the ground itself when it chooses to: a low resonant common-tongue felt in the chest before it is heard in the ear. The conversations are rare and remembered. Eats the soil and what is in it, including ore-veins, including buried things, including the dead. A confirmed kill is a generational event. Most encounters are not kills.

Centaur-thane.text: Centaur-thane: Wartide-bred centauroid. Horse-bodied, humanoid torso, bull-thick through the chest and shoulders—heavier and crueler than the elegant centaurs of older fables. Roams the back-country of the Long Steppe and Reach margins in bands of three to five led by a thane. Speaks well, with the formality of an old soldiery, and the thane will parley before a fight if the parley promises better return than the fight. Takes captives of either sex on raids and breeds them in a back-camp for stock. Bands fightable on the open ground; the thane is the boss kill, armored, heavy axe, war-cry that staggers anyone within fifty paces.

Tarkle.text: slimy tentacular plant-things that infest areas; tarkle-infestations and Tarklewoods are real places of trouble. Not intelligent but mobile and aggressive.

Grist.text: Wartide-remnant scavengers. Knee-high, round and furry, scaly grey, with a small mouth. Travel in packs of four to twenty, raid grain stores, occasionally pull down a child and gnaw at earlobes and the tips of fingers. Every village has grist stories. They are more a nuisance than a threat.

Skink.text: reptilian rat-equivalents, dog-sized, that can take on humans in combat in theory, but prefer to flee if threatened.

Fenkin.text: Small, semi-amphibious humanoids of the marshes. Failed ensoulment, maybe — frog-souled almost-people who didn't quite become a race. Tribal, hostile, dangerous in their own waters, manageable on dry land. Goblin-equivalent with unintelligible speech. Known to capture females and hold them in their lairs as breeding stock or for sport.

Magic.text: The channeling of the world's concentrated living power through body and will — a well-known fact of life, with few wielders. Systems vary by race; not all races have magic. Human magic is gendered and asymmetric. Men generate mana — a steady, modest output that recovers overnight. Women generate none but can store vastly more than any man can hold; a trained witch with a full reservoir can outmatch a circle of wizards. The mechanism of filling is conveyed through male essence — kept hidden from rank-and-file wizards, who are taught 'channeling' as a discipline of focused will. Only masters and archmagi are told on elevation, by which point they are too deep in to walk away. Witches know, because filling is something they experience directly. Witches. Outlawed in every human kingdom outside Avenor; doctrinally classed as apostates rather than malign. Day-to-day texture is suppression and fear rather than open hunt — a discreet hedge-witch can live her whole life unbothered, but one who reveals herself is arrested, tried, exiled, or quietly killed without public proceeding. In Avenor the practice is open, lawful, and dignified through contractual consort-wizard donor lines at the Queen's College, supplemented by extraction from magical creatures. Outside Avenor, witches fill from cooperating men where they can or from black-market mana potions whose origins range from licensed Avenorian production to outright atrocity — the unicorn trade and other beast-source extractions feed the high end. The Brotherhood of Wizards. Austere, martial, openly antagonistic to the witch line, celibate by doctrine — bonds between brothers permitted, contact with women forbidden. Seated at the High Forum of Telinor in Kelmar, with lesser Forums in the other human kingdoms held under treaty as parallel institutions outside royal authority. Boys with the spark are taken to the agoge at seven or eight and trained brutally — magical and physical at once — into men who can hold their own with veteran soldiers and outclass them with the staff. Wizard magic is physical and applied. No fireballs, no lightning bolts. The working comes through the swing and the strike — force, hardening, motion, breaking, warding. A wizard drives a staff-strike through plate that should have stopped it, stands a wall against a charge that should have broken him, calls wind to slow a volley, stops a blade in mid-arc. The battlestaff is the wizard's standard combat instrument — Brotherhood-issued or smith-bought, replaceable and upgradeable, ranging from apprentice training staff to master's staff to the rare pre-Ensoulment relic. Each upgrade is a real moment in a wizard's career — procurement and recognition of rank, not ceremony. Their stated virtue: we do not throw fire, we break shields and we stand walls; let the witches show off. It is mostly a lie — at the ceiling, witches outmatch them — but it is a sustaining lie, and the senior conspiracy lets the rank and file believe it. Wizards are deployed with kingdom armies as a normal arm of war (answering to garrison commanders, never to the king) and run regional witch-cases as ordinary garrison work. They are most worth their cost against creatures and races that turn aside steel — the Threshi above all. Kings who would rather not host a Forum host one anyway, because the alternative is sending farmboys against things that eat farmboys. Non-human magic does not share the gender-split: Prime ancestor-magic is a slow conversation with the named dead, anchored to monastery bone-vaults and useless on the road; Molvar speak to roots and growing things; Cephalen master color-shifting deception as cultural discipline; Vorok prospectors feel ore beneath the ground. Each race's tradition is its own.

Human.text: Human: The pre-Ensoulment race of Hedoria, once dominant across the continent, now reduced to the peripheral kingdoms of Avenor, Udorath, Nadorim, and Kelmar. Median height — shorter than Hofnar, Prime, or Draklid; taller than Molvar. Lifespan around seventy years. Coloring and build vary by region: pale and dark-haired in Udorath, ruddy and broad in Kelmar, fairer and lighter-built in Nadorim, mixed in Avenor. The only race on the continent that does not trace its origins to Avenoria's working. Outside Avenor, humans live in stratified hereditary monarchies founded by Wartide survivors, agriculturally dependent on Quelled livestock and culturally defined by grievance over lost territory. Dress is regional: Udorathi favor linen and wool in chalk-white and salt-blue; Kelmari wear heavy garments for cold and saddle, with house-colors at the shoulder; Nadorim go in plain tough cloth with waterfowl-feather trim. Humans are the only race whose magical tradition split by gender into the wizard and witch lines, a division most non-human races find peculiar. Outside Avenor, the witch line is illegal and hunted.

Hofnar.text: Hofnar: Equine-humanoid people of the open steppes, lifted in the Ensoulment from aurochs, wild horses, and the great grazers. Stand a head taller than humans, broad-shouldered and long-limbed, with the casual durability of a lineage built for distance. Faces are broader than human, prominent-cheeked, with wide-set dark eyes and a slight muzzle. Some lineages bear small temple-horns; others none. Hair grows as a true mane down the back of the neck, braided and beaded by clan tradition. Feet are hoofed — split or single by lineage. Males are barrel-chested and heavy; females are lighter, faster, and frequently take the foreman roles in caravan operations. Hofnar civilization moves: their capitals are wagon-cities of hundreds of carved, painted clan-wagons drawn by ox-equivalents, following established seasonal routes between northern and southern grasslands. They build no fixed cities. Society is organized by clan, confederated into seven recognized Riderings. Status rests on lineage, deeds, and standing in trade; a Hofnar's word and contract-history define them. Education is practical — children learn roads, currencies, and customs before letters. They regard the wild far-herds of the deep steppe as the Grandfathers and never hunt them.

Cephalen.text: Cephalen: Cephalopod-descended people of the coastal cities, lifted from octopus and squid in the Ensoulment. Broadly humanoid in silhouette but soft and near-boneless, capable of compressing through gaps a third their apparent size. Skin is smooth and rubbery in pearl, slate, and iridescent tones, shifting color involuntarily with emotion and intent — a Cephalen at peace is muted, a Cephalen lying flickers visibly to anyone trained to read them. Other Cephalen always read them. Outsiders almost never do. They bruise badly, take crushing damage poorly, and dry out without regular immersion. Their port-capitals are half-submerged — upper streets for foreign business, lower wet-galleries for Cephalen life. Society is organized into merchant houses with deep familial structures and elaborate codes of obligation. They are unfailingly polite, formally hospitable, and considered by every other race to be impossible to fully trust. Cephalen do not lie outright — the skin betrays them — but they have refined the art of selective truth across generations. They hold contracts to the letter, no further. They favor flowing robes, layered silks, and jewelry worked from pearl, coral, and sea-metal.

Draklid.text: Draklid: Drake-descended people of the high desert and broken plateau, lifted in the Ensoulment from the lesser drakes of the interior. Tall, lean, sinewy, with the long-limbed build of a predator made for hot open country and explosive short-range pursuit. Scales run in warm tones — rust, copper, ochre, deep gold, sand, dark bronze, occasionally black edged with bronze — patterned along the flanks in clan-specific markings. Faces have a long narrow muzzle; a fan of curving horns sweeps back from brow and temples. Eyes are slit-pupilled in amber, yellow, or deep red. Long powerful tails are used constantly for balance and emphasis — a Draklid whose tail goes still has made a decision. Small vestigial wings fold against the back, expressive in display, useless for flight. Long-lived; a Draklid elder may have known their great-grandparents personally. They organize into small sovereign clan-holds — each clan its own proud nation, no clan ever willingly subordinate to another. No outside power has unified them. The Draklid prefer it this way. They are scrupulously fair in trade and lethal in offense; their reputation for measured, final retribution travels ahead of them. Dress is layered silks and linens in deep crimson, cream, and gold, with heavy gilt ornament and clan sigils worked into belt and lapel.

Prime.text: Prime: The eldest race of Hedoria, already a people when Avenoria began her working. Taller than humans, heavily built for cold and altitude, with bear-like trunks and long arms. Bodies are covered in dense white fur thickening at chest, neck, and tail. Faces are pale and expressive, closer in proportion to a chimpanzee's than a bear's, with a long lion-like muzzle giving the face its forward sweep. Long fully-furred tails. Lifespans far exceed those of humans; Prime elders may have lived through the Wartide. They live in mountain monasteries that predate the modern continent, holding no trade routes, no fleets, and no territorial ambition beyond their high holdings. Their magical tradition is ancestor-magic — the bones of every Prime mage who has ever lived are kept and consulted across thousands of years. The wizard colleges are afraid of them. Avenor treats them as peers. They speak slowly, answer carefully, and refuse to discuss what they were before the Ensoulment. Dress is layered robes in deep blue, indigo, and crimson, heavy with embroidery, ancestor-tokens, and beaded prayer-strings. They carry carved staves of bone and wood. Few outsiders are admitted to the monasteries.

Molvar.text: Molvar: Burrowing tuber-people of the central continent, lifted in the Ensoulment from the deep root-systems and loam-dwelling things of the soil. Stout, low-slung, broad-shouldered and heavy through the middle, walking on four short root-legs ending in rounded blunt tips. Two stubby root-arms emerge from the upper body, ending in clusters of finger-rootlets capable of fine work. Flesh is plant-tuber tissue beneath thick rind-skin in shades of pale brown, ochre, warm umber, and deep earthy red, often subtly patterned along the flanks. Faces are pig-and-mole in proportion but unmistakably intelligent: bright forward-set eyes, broad expressive mouth, sensory tendrils where whiskers would be. Plumpness is admired; fatness is admired more; a skinny Molvar is a Molvar something has gone wrong with. They live in wide, brightly lit tunnel networks beneath meadow and forest edge, shored with living root, decorated with mushroom gardens and oil lamps. Cuisine is rich, slow, root-heavy. Their fermented mushroom liquors and fungal wines travel poorly. Drunkenness is a mark of pride. They have no aptitude for continental magical traditions but possess a deep affinity for roots and plants — closer to gardening than sorcery, but going well beyond what gardening explains. Hospitable, warm, and famously difficult to dislodge from a tunnel they know.

Threshi.text: Threshi: Locust-and-mantis people of the central lowlands, a wartide race lifted from the bulk hosts the Wartide had bred for its campaigns. The most numerous people in Hedoria. Stand taller than a human, narrow-bodied, chitin-plated in dust-browns, tans, pale ochres, and sand-yellows. Triangular heads with large compound eyes, mandibles that fold inward to speak and outward to eat, antennae trailing back along the skull. Segmented wing-panels fold along the back at rest, capable of short flights and long glides rather than sustained flight. Voices are clicking and dry. Strongly sexually dimorphic. Females are larger and heavier, with chitin-shovel forelimbs that break ground no human plow could turn — they hold the land, manage the harvest, and run the household estates. Males are smaller, faster, and built for aggression; they ride out at every season's end in war-bands to take meat and ground from neighboring kingdoms. The Threshi breed rapidly and have more mouths than their farmland can feed in any but the best years. They are aristocratic and hierarchical, organized around warlord-houses with male warlord and female consort-managers sharing real power. The King is always male, always a great warrior; no Threshi king has died peacefully. They cannot keep livestock — any household that tries has its herd stolen by other Threshi within a season — so they raid the human marches for Quelled instead.

Raknid.text: Raknid: The redeemed wartide race — the spider brood-line that broke from its kin during Avenoria's working and turned on the rest of the Wartide. Stand taller than humans, lightly built, with long limbs and a slight frame. Skin is pale chitin, plated along the torso and limbs in articulated segments, smooth and faintly cool. Beneath the human-set arms, two further legs descend from the lower body — long, jointed, ending in fine clawed points — so that a Raknid walks in a four-beat gait on four spidery legs. Faces have six eyes set in two arcs: an upper pair large and humanlike, four lower eyes smaller and clustered. The mouth carries small fanged chelicerae, folded close to the lips when not in use. From the scalp grows a fine webbed mantle of pale silken fibers, half hair and half spun thread. Voices are soft and slightly clicking. Long-lived, comparable to Draklid. They have no homeland — the high country their parent race ran was scoured during the Wartide and is uninhabited still. They live in scattered small communities of a few dozen to a few hundred, in hill country, forest margins, and back valleys other peoples have not bothered to take. They farm, craft, and build modestly. They take work that rewards patience: scholars, archivists, copyists, linguists, healers, occasional witches. Most Raknid communities have been burned out at least once in living memory, and they teach their children against returning violence under any circumstance short of immediate physical defense.

Vorok.text: Vorok: Mining people of Hedoria, a wartide race whose precursors were bred by the dark powers as labor — heavy bodies built to dig, haul, and break stone in the great fortifications. Stand taller than humans, with a silhouette that varies by trade across a lifetime; the body adapts to the work. A Vorok at the seam-face for decades grows broad and slab-shouldered with thick chest and arms; a Vorok who works as foreman or principal stays lean and finishes narrow-built. The same lineage produces both. Faces are heavy-jawed with prominent lower tusks, broad brow, deep-set eyes, and large pointed ears that fold flat in concentration or anger. Hair is coarse and grows in a stiff crest from crown to neck, shaved short for work or kept long for standing. Skin runs in dull tones — grey, slate, smoky blue, dusty orange, ochre — with no consistent pattern; two siblings can come out very different colors. Voices are deep and slow, with a grinding accent. Lifespan is roughly that of humans. There are no Vorok women — reproduction is contractual, conducted in breeding compounds adjoining each operation, with bearers under fixed-term contracts. The race is organized into trading consortiums called houses, which hold ore-works contracts in every kingdom that will sign them. The kingdoms dislike them and renew the contracts. They dress practically: heavy work-leathers and tool-rigs for the pit, long coats and waistcoats with house-sigils worked into metal at the lapel for foremen and principals.

Fernwarg.text: Fernwarg: Wolf-derived forest people of the great central forests, lifted in the Ensoulment from the wolf-packs and their fern-grown country. Tall upright humanoids with the build of a lean wolf-runner — long-limbed, narrow-waisted, taller than humans and lighter, with digitigrade clawed feet and long-fingered clawed hands. Faces are unmistakably wolf-derived: long muzzle, erect mobile ears, forward-set amber or yellow eyes, jaws built for the bite. The coat is the central fact of their appearance — a fern-fronded growth of fur in deep green, moss, and forest-dark, fine-divided and overlapping along back, shoulders, arms, and tail, with denser undercoat fur beneath. The mane runs from high shoulders down the spine, lifting in threat and display, woven by its bearer with bones, claws, and beads. Females are visibly larger and heavier-built than males, with denser, more elaborate manes. Pack-mothers wear ceremonial mane-headdresses of layered dark fronds. Males are leaner and smaller. Society is matriarchal and pack-organized; pack-mothers rule, manage diplomacy, and arrange the contracts under which young males travel abroad as mercenaries. Females rarely leave the forest. A Fernwarg standing still in summer bracken is nearly invisible. They speak common-tongue with a slight rolling cadence the muzzle gives every language, and they refuse to return Quelled escapees who have crossed into their forests.

Quelled.text: Quelled: The youngest race of Hedoria, lifted in the Ensoulment from the domesticated animals that lived alongside humanity before the Wartide — pigs, cattle, sheep, and farm fowl. The working caught them most completely and most recently, and they did not rise as far from their precursors as the other ensouled races. Several distinct lineages emerged from the same working. Pig-quell are broad and stout, with flat snouts and short bristled hair. Cow-quell are tall and powerfully built, with long faces, broad nostrils, and short horns in many lineages. Sheep-quell are smaller, fleece-haired, and soft-faced. Fowl-quell are leaner and sharper-featured, with feather-down across scalp and arms. Bodies are humanoid in plan but heavy with animal heritage; not all stand fully upright. Hands are functional but coarse, feet flat or hoofed depending on lineage, voices deep or clipped. The domesticated animals were caught completely — there are no longer any unensouled pigs, cattle, sheep, or farm fowl in Hedoria. The Quelled have produced no civilization of their own. Their tongues are crude, their oldest songs reach back perhaps four generations, and they have no written letters, shared history, or craft tradition outside what they perform under direction. A Quell raised in a working household is dull and biddable, slow to speak, given to long silences and simple repetitive labor. Whether more is in them than this is a question few in the human kingdoms ever think to ask.

The Wartide.text: The Wartide: A confederation of dark powers that came to Hedoria centuries ago, varied in form and origin and united by appetite. The Wartide was a war of attrition — overwhelming numerical and material force, monstrous armies, corrupted lands, and plague-tides. It killed everything in its path, took territory and held it, and produced more of itself faster than its enemies could kill it. Human kingdoms fell one after another. Conventional resistance failed: the wizard organisations of the era could not match its rate of attrition, the older non-human races could not coordinate, and the Primes held their mountain monasteries but could not project force outward. The war was broken by Avenoria's working — the Ensoulment — but not destroyed. The great hosts were shattered, but fragments remained. The Hollows are where those fragments concentrate, and they are still active. Smaller Wartide creatures fled the killing and bred in the wild places. The evil is broken but not gone.

The Ensoulment.text: The Ensoulment: The great working performed by the first witch queen, Avenoria, that broke the back of the Wartide and remade the moral architecture of the continent. Faced with the Wartide's overwhelming numerical advantage, Avenoria's response was not redemption-magic but mass force-creation. She gathered every ounce of mana available — the cooperating wizards of her era and the stockpiles of the wizard institutions she could reach — and used it to lift everything she could touch into people who could fight: forests, herds, predators of the wild, animals on the edge of the killing-fields, the dark forces themselves where she could reach them. The lifted became the ensouled races. Some of the Wartide hosts the working touched broke under it and turned against their kin — these are the wartide races that now occupy an uncomfortable place in Hedoria. The Ensoulment was not done for the newly ensouled's own sake. The places and creatures that became peoples were lifted because Avenoria needed soldiers; the fact that the lifted were now people with their own claims and futures was a consequence of the working, not its purpose. Avenor's official history frames the Ensoulment as gift and salvation. The historical record of what Avenoria actually did is unambiguous: there was a war on, she was losing it, and she did this because she needed an army.

Avenoria the First Witch Queen.text: Avenoria: The first witch queen, performer of the Ensoulment, founder of Avenor. She is the most contested figure in Hedorian history. Avenor venerates her as saviour. The other human kingdoms regard her as the woman who saved humanity by giving away its world. The standard human criticism: she performed the working without consulting the human kingdoms who were her allies, lifted races into being without knowing what they would become or what they would claim, failed to defend humanity's claims when those new peoples demanded land in compensation, and is responsible for every soul-war fought since. The defence: without her, no one would be alive to have this conversation. The Wartide would have killed everything on the continent. The lifted races earned their lands by defending them. The post-war settlement was rough but inevitable — armies that win wars do not give back the territory they bled for. Both arguments are partly true. Hedoria has been arguing them for centuries and shows no sign of resolving them. A person's view of Avenoria tells you almost everything about their politics. The wizard organisations carry their own grievance: their precursors lost authority in the working, were drawn upon and superseded, and have been permanently diminished since. They hold that her working was hubris and that conventional wizardry could have eventually turned the Wartide back without it.

The Aftermath.text: The Aftermath: The political settlement following the Wartide. Humanity emerged from the war scattered and weakened. Cities had fallen, kingdoms had collapsed, populations had been halved or worse, and the hereditary nobility had been decimated. The new races took the lands they had fought on, and the lands no humans were left to hold — forests, steppes, coastal waters, broken country, frontier hills. From the ensouled races' perspective, this was fair reward for fighting the war they had been called into. From the human perspective, it was catastrophic loss; half the continent had been theirs before the Wartide. The human realms that exist today are reconstructions, built in the decades after the war by survivors. Their pre-war territory was mostly gone — much overrun by the Wartide and now Hollow or wilderness, much held by the new races during and after the war, much simply depopulated and never reclaimed. Most humans alive today do not think about the Wartide much; it was centuries ago. What they think about is the lost territory, the wars their grandparents fought against the new races, and the kingdoms that no longer exist.

The Hollows.text: The Hollows: The territories where Wartide fragments concentrated after the great hosts were broken. The Hollows were not cleansed. They are still active. The Hollows lie across the continent in patches of varying size — some a few miles across, some larger than human kingdoms — and the things in them have not been counted, killed, or contained. Smaller Wartide creatures bred in the wild places after the war, and lineages of corrupted beasts persist in deep forest, broken country, and abandoned battlefield. Most border kingdoms maintain wardens and bounty registries against incursions. None claim the Hollows as territory.

The First Soul-War.text: The First Soul-War: The first war fought over the post-Wartide settlement, perhaps fifty years after the Ensoulment, when the reconstructed human kingdoms tried to retake the great forests they had lost. They failed. The Fernwarg held the forest country and have held it since. Human armies broke themselves against Molvar tunnel networks and accomplished nothing they could hold. The territories the ensouled races claimed in the aftermath of the Wartide are the territories they still hold, and the First Soul-War is what proved they could keep them.

The Second Soul-War.text: The Second Soul-War: The second war fought over the post-Wartide settlement, roughly a century after the Ensoulment, fought between humans and the steppe peoples over the eastern grasslands. It was inconclusive, but the new races retained most of what they held. Relations between the steppe peoples and the human kingdoms have been wary ever since.

The Third Soul-War.text: The Third Soul-War: The war most expect to see in their lifetimes. The pressure has been building for a generation. Drivers include human nationalist movements pushing for renewed expansion into the forests, the Brotherhood at Telinor seeking the suppression of the witch line and the Queen's College, and Udorath's demand for Avenor's river-valley as agricultural territory. The Fernwarg pack-mothers have been quietly coordinating with the Raknid elders for the first time in a century. Neither forest people wants the war. Both are preparing for it. The Hofnar Riderings have begun pre-positioning supply caches along their migration routes. The witch queen's diplomatic corps is busy. The third soul-war is regarded by all parties as a likely future event rather than an active present conflict, but military and diplomatic preparations are ongoing on multiple sides.

Avenor.text: Avenor: The southern coastal kingdom founded by Avenoria after the Wartide, sole human realm where the witch line is legal and the original cooperative magical tradition is preserved. Ruled by the witch queen, Avenoria's successor and namesake by title. Avenor recognises Raknid standing under the queen's writ, has outlawed the trade in Quelled for two generations, and shelters Quelled escapees who reach its borders. Diplomatically, Avenor maintains formal peer relations with the Primes and treats with the Fernwarg pack-mothers and Raknid elders. The other three human kingdoms regard Avenor as an apostate state and are formally allied against it. Avenor's river-valley is the most productive grain region in human Hedoria; Udorath claims it as historical human territory. Religious observance is nominal — Hand-days are kept as state ceremony and old Hand-temples remain in use, but the faith is not central to public life. This is the principal religious dispute between Avenor and the other kingdoms.

Udorath.text: Udorath: Western coastal human kingdom on the Whitestone, named for the chalk cliffs that face the sea. A hereditary monarchy with a strong landed nobility seated in the chalk uplands and along the salt-roads. Pre-Wartide Udorath was larger; an engineered system of dikes, levees, and drainage works had extended its arable land over centuries. The Wartide destroyed the works and the labor needed to maintain them. The land was not reclaimed, and the marsh and sea returned to most of it. Building foundations of flooded villages remain visible at low tide. Modern Udorath produces sheep-quell and cereal from the chalk uplands, grain from surviving fen-edge farmland, reed and salt from the marshes, linen flax regarded as the highest-quality on the continent, smoked eel and waterfowl, and finished iron from small bog-iron smelters. Foreign policy is dominated by the question of farmland — Avenor's river-valley is the active object. Religious observance is moderate and stoic in tone; Hand-days are kept and the capital's Hand-temple is old and active. Udorath drives the anti-Avenor alliance most actively.

Kelmar.text: Kelmar: Northern human kingdom at the head of the Shieldcap mountains, holding the line against the Threshi marches. Hereditary monarchy with a martial culture and the principal military force among the human kingdoms. Kelmari produce horse-stock, heavy iron, hardwood from the upland forests, and the salted and smoked meats their cold months require. Kelmar's marches lords face the Threshi raid cycle every winter and have done so for three centuries; the marches forces are the most experienced field army in human Hedoria. The Telinor wizards maintain a permanent presence at Shield's End, where wizards bless seasonal levies and faith is integrated with military culture. Religious observance is martial. The Kelmari court is formally allied against Avenor but has so far declined Telinor and Udorathi proposals for action — the marches lords oppose any redeployment from the Threshi line, and a campaign west would mean uncovering the eastern frontier.

Nadorim.text: Nadorim: Northeastern human kingdom in the lake-country, the smallest of the human kingdoms by population and arable land. Hereditary monarchy with a maritime and riverine economy. Nadorim produces freshwater fish, waterfowl, oiled leathers, reed-paper, and the boats their lake-trade depends on. Religious observance is the highest among the human kingdoms — Hand-shrines stand at every pier, boats pause before departing, and the Hand is referenced in casual speech. A rotating Telinor wizard detachment is received warmly and considered a mark of honor on the realm. Nadorim does not drive the anti-Avenor alliance and is expected to follow Udorath and Kelmar's lead. The kingdom maintains active road-and-river trade with both other human realms but has minimal direct contact with the Cephalen.

The Brotherhood at Telinor.text: The Brotherhood at Telinor: The post-Wartide wizard institution of the three non-Avenor human kingdoms, headquartered at Telinor and operating a presence in every realm that will host them. The Brotherhood's founding grievance is the Ensoulment — its precursor institutions were drawn on, superseded, and permanently diminished by Avenoria's working, and the Brotherhood holds that conventional wizardry could have turned the Wartide back without the lifting. Modern Brotherhood positions: the witch line is illegitimate, the Queen's College at Avenor is heretical, and the original cooperative tradition Avenor claims to preserve never existed. The Brotherhood trains wizards through the agoge — a rigorous decade-long program that produces the dark-robed staff-bearing figures recognized across the continent. Telinor maintains permanent detachments at Shield's End in Kelmar and rotating detachments in Nadorim, blessing levies, advising rulers, and conducting the quiet hunts against witches that the three kingdoms officially do not discuss. The Brotherhood is the most consistent driver of anti-Avenor policy in human Hedoria.

The Witch Line.text: The Witch Line: The female-line magical tradition of human Hedoria, descended from Avenoria. Witchcraft is illegal in Udorath, Kelmar, and Nadorim, where hunts are conducted quietly rather than publicly and captured witches are tried and disposed of without public proceeding. The witch line is legal in Avenor, where the Queen's College at the capital trains witches openly. Avenor's position is that the witch line is the original cooperative tradition and that the post-Wartide Brotherhood broke from it. The Brotherhood's position is that the witch line is heretical innovation. The two positions have been in stalemate since the Wartide ended. A witch is visually indistinguishable from any other woman, which is the practical heart of the problem.

The Queen's College.text: The Queen's College: The witches' institution at Avenor, founded by Avenoria and maintained by every witch queen since. The Queen's College trains witches openly, archives the working that ended the Wartide, and provides the magical corps of the Avenorian state. The College is the institutional heart of the witch line and the principal target of Brotherhood doctrine. Suppression of the Queen's College is among the founding aims of the Brotherhood at Telinor and one of the active drivers of anti-Avenor pressure in the three kingdoms.

The Quelled Trade.text: The Quelled Trade: The agricultural foundation of human civilization outside Avenor. Quelled are bred, raised, and worked on licensed farms across Udorath, Kelmar, and Nadorim. Their meat, wool, and milk are staples of common diet; their labor is used in field, mill, and household work. The trade is run by chartered houses under royal license, supplied by breeding farms, and regulated by overseers; operating practices follow the patterns of pre-Ensoulment livestock husbandry. The human kingdoms' arable lands cannot feed their populations on grain and vegetables alone, and the Ensoulment took every other source of meat, milk, and wool. The trade has settled into the rhythms of ordinary life. Rural humans grew up around the system and learned the local euphemisms in childhood; urban humans rarely see the slaughterhouses. Avenor outlawed the practice two generations ago. Quelled who escape estates in the three kingdoms are pursued; those who reach Avenor are received under the queen's writ. The Hofnar transport Quelled under contract — one of the moral compromises of the Hofnar position. Progressive Hofnar families have refused the work for a generation; traditional families take it as ordinary commerce.

The Riderings.text: The Riderings: The seven major Hofnar confederations, each a federation of clans sharing migration routes, intermarriage rights, and customary trading territory. Each Rideing has its own dialect, traditions, and seasonal cycle, and dozens of smaller bands operate within or alongside them. The Riderings are the political units of Hofnar civilization — there is no Hofnar king or central court. Ride business is conducted in moots held at established encampment sites along each Rideing's route, with senior matriarchs and patriarchs from member clans attending. Inter-Rideing matters are settled by the Great Moot, called rarely and held at neutral ground. The Riderings are currently divided over the chattel contracts: progressive families have refused the Quelled trade for a generation, traditional families take it as ordinary commerce. The split runs through clans, through marriages, sometimes through individual conscience. A formal split between progressive and traditional Riderings has been predicted for years and has not yet come.

Hofnar Wagon-Cities.text: The Wagon-Cities: The migratory capitals of the Hofnar Riderings. Each great Rideing maintains a wagon-city of hundreds of vast clan-wagons drawn by ox-equivalents, arranged in formation when encamped, with central avenues of beaten earth, market squares, ceremonial fires, and the embroidered tents of dignitaries. A Hofnar capital at rest is unmistakable on the horizon — bright with banners, painted in clan colors, ordered with a precision that surprises foreign visitors who expect nomad camps to be chaotic. The wagons themselves are works of generations. A great clan-wagon may be the size of a small castle, sides carved and painted with the deeds of ancestors, interior lined with hide, felt, and embroidered hangings, furniture built to fold and travel. The wealthiest wagons have multiple levels, carved verandas, and gilt fittings on their yokes. A clan's wagon is its hall, its temple, its archive, and its history. To lose a clan-wagon is the most catastrophic possible blow to a Hofnar lineage; clans that have lost theirs spend generations rebuilding standing. Wagon-cities follow predictable seasonal cycles between northern and southern grasslands, with established encampments at established sites. Foreign envoys and trading partners know where the great Hofnar capitals will be in any given season and travel to meet them there.

The Cephalen Houses.text: The Cephalen Houses: The merchant families that govern the Cephalen port-capitals. The Houses are extended kin-networks held together by elaborate codes of obligation that other races find almost impossible to read from outside — debts and counter-debts running across generations, marriage contracts that bind not couples but lineages, fosterage arrangements that move children between households for reasons foreigners are rarely told. Each port-capital is governed by its assembled Houses through a council whose proceedings are held in lower wet-galleries that no non-Cephalen has ever attended. House standing is built on contracts honored, debts collected, blood-bonds maintained, and territorial concessions held. Inter-House politics is conducted with the same scrupulous formal courtesy the Cephalen show outsiders, and is no easier to read. The Houses agree on one thing without exception: the Cephalen monopoly on Hedorian sea trade is non-negotiable, and any rival fleet that attempts the open water is dealt with by joint action. They took the seas through blockade and force two centuries ago. They have held them since.

The Cephalen Sea Monopoly.text: The Cephalen Sea Monopoly: Cephalen ships carry everything that moves by sea in Hedoria. The monopoly was taken by force two centuries ago, when the assembled Cephalen Houses broke every other maritime power on the continent in a sequence of campaigns the survivors do not like to discuss. It has been held since by a combination of overwhelming naval superiority, an exclusive treaty network with every coastal kingdom, and the practical fact that no one else has a fleet anymore. Foreign ships are permitted under license — fishing rights, coastal cabotage, river trade above tidewater — and operate under a tariff regime calibrated to ensure they never grow large enough to challenge the Houses. Any vessel that attempts open-water trade without Cephalen carriage is intercepted; the polite term is escorted. The Houses prefer not to sink ships when extracting tribute will do, but the option remains open and is exercised regularly enough that the lesson does not need repeating.

Draklid Clan-Holds.text: Draklid Clan-Holds: The sovereign mountain-and-plateau territories of the Draklid clans. Each clan-hold is its own nation — its own walls, laws, treaties, and feuds. There are no greater Draklid polities. There has never been a Draklid king, emperor, or confederation that lasted past the death of its founder. Outside powers wishing to deal with the Draklid must negotiate one clan at a time, and a contract with one clan-hold confers nothing in the territory of another. The clans are intensely status-conscious; honor-debts run across generations, and clan-hold against clan-hold rivalries are tracked with archival precision. Inter-clan disputes are settled by formal challenge, ritual combat, or arbitration by neutral elders, and the ruling stands. Foreign attempts to conquer or unify the Draklid have been numerous over the centuries and uniformly disastrous; a Draklid clan-hold is small, ferocious, and impossible to reduce without committing forces the invader cannot afford to lose, and a victory over one clan-hold simply commits the invader to fighting all the others. The Draklid prefer it this way.

The Prime Monasteries.text: The Prime Monasteries: The mountain holdings of the Primes, fortress-temples and ancestor-archives that have stood since before the modern continent. The monasteries are not open to outsiders. Few foreigners have been admitted in living memory, and what is inside them is described in Hedorian scholarship through second-hand reports and Prime statements that explain little. What is known: each monastery houses an ancestor-archive — the bones of every Prime mage who has ever served there, kept and consulted across thousands of years. The archives are the foundation of Prime magic. The wizard colleges of human Hedoria regard them with a fear that the colleges do not articulate publicly. Avenor maintains formal peer relations with the senior monasteries and is the only outside power treated as such. The Primes hold no trade routes, project no fleets, and contend for no territory beyond their high country. Their political doctrine is a refusal to be drawn into continental wars — a refusal they have maintained through every major conflict since the Wartide, including the Wartide itself, in which they held their mountains and could not be reached.

Vorok Houses.text: Vorok Houses: The mining consortiums that govern Vorok civilization. There is no Vorok kingdom or homeland in any conventional sense — the homeland the Wartide built them in was abandoned generations ago — and the Houses are the political and economic units of Vorok life. Each House is a corporate-clan structure holding contracts, operations, breeding compounds, and a registered membership of enrolled Voroks across the continent. Houses negotiate with foreign powers, hold concessions, train apprentices, settle disputes between members, and discipline their own. Inter-House compacts govern poaching, contract-jumping, and the blacklisting of expelled members. A Vorok in good standing with any House can find work in any operation; an exiled Vorok blacklisted across the compacts cannot work in consortium operations anywhere on the continent. There are perhaps a dozen great Houses and many smaller ones, with constant churn at the margins. The greatest Houses — Trenmark, Iresunder, Hod-Velagh, and others — operate concessions in every kingdom that signs their contracts. Their principals are recognizable on sight: lean narrow-built Voroks in long coats and waistcoats, with house-sigils in metal at lapel and belt, doing the work of contract negotiation with a reserve that foreign clients sometimes mistake for stupidity. They are not stupid.

Vorok Reproduction.text: Vorok Reproduction: Vorok reproduction is contractual. There are no Vorok women, and the working that lifted them did not produce a sex that could bear them. The Houses maintain breeding compounds adjoining their major operations — decent housing with kitchens, gardens, and medical staff, despite the laborer slang that calls them pits — staffed by women under fixed-term contracts of bearing. The standard contract specifies term length, frequency of coupling, number of bearings expected, accommodation, medical provision, payment schedule, and release terms. Voroks are not very fertile and frequent coupling is expected; the contracts account for it explicitly and the rates reflect it. Most contracted bearers are Quelled, sold to Vorok houses through the southern markets where the trade is legal. A smaller number are women of other races — humans down on their luck, the occasional Threshi dissident, Hofnar in catastrophic debt — who sign on for the high rates the work commands. A few seek the work directly. The Houses do not advertise the arrangement and do not discuss it with foreign clients. It is not secret in any meaningful sense — anyone who asks is told the basic structure — but it is not a feature of the brand. There are persistent rumors of less voluntary contracts, of bearers held past their term, of compounds in the deeper galleries where the formal terms are not honored. The Houses deny these rumors. Some are denied more convincingly than others. A Vorok child is raised in the compound until adolescence and is then placed with a House crew as apprentice.

Free Vorok Crews.text: Free Vorok Crews: Voroks operating outside House enrollment. A small number of free crews stay independent and honest, working at small scale on contracts the Houses scorn. They are tolerated. The Houses regard them as a species of failure. Many free crews drift into banditry — the skills are transferable, and a crew that can blast a tunnel can blast a road. Vorok bandits are a recognized hazard along the old homeland frontiers and in the back country between kingdoms, particularly along the trade routes the Houses do not service. The Houses do not pursue Vorok bandits and do not consider unenrolled Voroks their problem; foreign authorities that complain are quoted on the cost of contracted security. A House that wants a particular Vorok genuinely expunged from the system can revoke his enrollment and share his name across the inter-House compacts. The blacklisted Vorok cannot work in any consortium operation anywhere on the continent. Most blacklisted exiles leave Vorok society entirely — caravan guards, mining-camp consultants for foreign operations the Houses have not reached, occasional bodyguards for foreign principals who want a Vorok's particular register without contracting with a House.

Threshi Raid Cycle.text: The Threshi Raid Cycle: The seasonal pattern of Threshi war-band raids against the human kingdoms, conducted every year for three centuries. Threshi unattached males ride out at every season's end to take more land and meat from neighboring kingdoms — some at the warlord's direction, settling disputes between noble houses or pressing on a contested border, others in small groups or alone seeking land they can hold and a female who will join them on it. Some go for Quelled; the Threshi need meat and have never managed to keep livestock, so raids for Quelled are about as common as raids for land. Spring and summer are quiet — the war-bands are home for the planting and the early growing. Autumn tenses with the harvest. Winter brings the bands, when the food is in storage and the males are free to ride. By spring they have fallen back to plow. Any single band can be broken by a competent human force, and organized resistance scatters them. But the bands are continuous, and the season does not end. Three centuries of inches has put a great quantity of formerly human land under Threshi grain. Significant territory is still in active dispute. The Kelmari marches are the heaviest line.

Fernwarg Pack-Mothers.text: Pack-Mothers: The matriarchal authorities of Fernwarg society. Each pack is led by its pack-mother — typically the eldest competent female in the pack's senior line, though competence outweighs seniority and a poor pack-mother can be challenged. Pack-mothers manage the pack's territory, arrange the contracts under which young males travel abroad as mercenaries, conduct diplomacy with neighboring packs, and represent the pack to outside powers. Inter-pack matters are settled by councils of pack-mothers held at established forest meeting-grounds. There is no Fernwarg king, queen, or central court. The pack-mothers have been quietly coordinating with the Raknid elders for the first time in a century, and the witch queen's diplomatic corps has been receiving longer and more frequent pack-mother delegations in the last decade. Females rarely leave the forest. A Fernwarg pack-mother encountered abroad is conducting business serious enough to bring her out of her territory in person, and the people she is doing business with treat her accordingly.

Fernwarg Mercenary Tradition.text: Fernwarg Mercenary Tradition: The custom by which young Fernwarg males travel abroad to take mercenary work, sending their earnings home and returning to the forest after a contract season. Mercenary companies value Fernwarg males — fast in pursuit, terrible in close combat, and possessed of the absolute reliability that comes from a male trained from cubhood to honor the contracts his pack-mother makes for him. The work runs to caravan guard, scout, bodyguard in noble households, and occasional duelist. They charge fairly, work hard, and disappear when the contract ends. A Fernwarg encountered outside the forests is almost always male, almost always young, and almost always working. They wear human-style traveling clothes over the coat and carry weapons sized for their reach. They speak warmly of their pack-mothers and their sisters, and rarely of the females their own age. They do not, when asked, explain the laugh.

Raknid Persecution.text: Raknid Persecution: The continuing pattern of attacks on Raknid communities across Hedoria. The redemption of the Raknid during the Ensoulment is acknowledged in formal contexts — Avenorian law recognizes it, the older post-Wartide treaties reference it — but in ordinary speech most of the continent collapses the distinction between Raknid and the rest of the Wartide. Spider-people, broodkin, the wartide-blood. A Raknid traveling alone through human country can expect to be refused service at most inns, to pay double the posted price, and to be followed out of any town they enter at dusk. Most Raknid communities have been burned out at least once in living memory. A village lives quietly for a generation, then a neighboring lord's child sickens, or a witch-hunter passes through, and the community is destroyed. The survivors scatter and rebuild somewhere else. The Raknid do not fight back. They teach their children against returning violence under any circumstance short of immediate physical defense, and the doctrine has held — there is no record of organized Raknid retaliation against any of the burnings. Avenor is the exception. The realm accepts Raknid refugees and recognizes their standing under the queen's writ. Raknid scholars staff the Queen's College's archive. The pack-mothers' recent coordination with Raknid elders is the first time in a century the two forest peoples have aligned.

The Hand.text: The Hand: The principal religious tradition of human Hedoria, observed across all four kingdoms in varying intensity. The Hand is understood as the guiding principle of cooperation, oath, and right relation between people — the open hand offered, the hand that holds the contract, the hand that strikes only when struck first. Hand-temples stand in every human capital and major town, with priests trained in arbitration as much as ceremony. Hand-days mark the seasonal turning points and the great moments of life — naming, partnering, burial. Observance varies by kingdom: Nadorim is the most devout, Kelmar's faith is martial and integrated with military culture, Udorath's is moderate and stoic, and Avenor's is nominal — Hand-days are kept as state ceremony and old Hand-temples remain in use, but the faith is not central to public life. This is the principal religious dispute between Avenor and the other kingdoms. The Hand has no central authority and no single doctrinal text; each kingdom's clergy maintains its own liturgical tradition.

The Anti-Avenor Alliance.text: The Anti-Avenor Alliance: The formal alliance between Udorath, Kelmar, and Nadorim against Avenor. The alliance is built around shared opposition to Avenor and mutual interest in trade and defense.
