The active drivers of anti-Avenor pressure are Udorath, which seeks Avenor's river-valley as agricultural territory, and the Brotherhood at Telinor, which seeks the suppression of the witch line and the Queen's College. Kelmar holds the principal military force but has not committed to a campaign west; the marches lords oppose any redeployment from the Threshi line, and the Kelmari court has so far declined Telinor and Udorathi proposals for action. Nadorim does not drive the alliance and is expected to follow Udorath and Kelmar's lead. The alliance is the institutional vehicle through which the third soul-war, when it comes, will most likely be declared.

Pre-Ensoulment Items.text:
Most enchanted items in Hedoria are old. Pre-Ensoulment human craft, recovered from ruins, family vaults, dwarf-style Vorok galleries, and the back-country sites the Wartide overran. Swords whose edge will not dull; rings that warm in cold weather; charms that ward small malice; scrolls that hold a single working until they are broken. The traditions that made them are mostly lost — pre-Ensoulment human magic was a different practice, and the post-Wartide split into the wizard and witch lines erased the working knowledge.

New enchantment is rare. Only strong witches manage it, the Queen's College trains a small number of enchanters each generation, and even there the work is slow and expensive — a useful charm costs hundreds of mana and days of focus to lay. A witch outside Avenor who can enchant is a quiet asset her village does not advertise.

The alchemy trade handles the practical side of old items. Identifying what an artifact does, how it activates, what it costs to use, and what fails about it is an apothecary's premium service. A merchant or lord who has come into a piece off a back-country dig pays an old apothecary in gold for an honest reading; the merchants who skip the reading sell into a market that will eventually find out and adjust the price. Most kingdoms regulate the trade in old items lightly. The Brotherhood claims jurisdiction over any enchanted weapon that turns up in an army's hands and is selectively enforcing about it.

Wizardry: the four working domains.text: Wizardry: the four working domains: Wizard apprentices at the agoge learn their working as four distinct domains of staff-applied force, each a fundamental form the channeled power takes through the wizard's body and will. Striking — the staff blow with the working behind it, driving through armor and shield that should have stopped it, force carried through the strike and released into the target. Bracing — holding ground that should have broken, standing a wall against cavalry charges and falling beams, the wizard's own weight made absolute and the press sustained until breaking. Warding — hardening self, ally, weapon, or armor against coming damage, a working sustained for a turn that makes the protected thing more resistant to harm. Motion — wind and force shaped through space, slowed volleys pulled from the air, blades stopped mid-arc, stepping where the ground will not hold. These four are the canon of Brotherhood teaching and the foundation of wizard practice. Wizardry does not project at distance in ordinary play — projection, the casting of force and light and flame at range, is the witches' domain and strength. This distinction is doctrinally important to the Brotherhood; it is also practically true. Witchcraft without a staff-focus is efficient at distance, while wizardry without the staff-work is inefficient at range, and the difference is built into the magic itself.

Witchcraft: the five domains.text: Witchcraft: the five domains: A trained witch's working repertoire falls into five practical schools, the bread-and-butter of witch practice across Hedoria and the foundation of Queen's College teaching. Projection — light, flame, cold, force at distance; witchlight conjured to see by, flame and cold called into being at range, lances of force hurled through space. Glamour — concealment and false impressions, the gaze that slides past, the eye that sees what the witch intends rather than what is true. Sight — scrying and intent-reading, divining the present moment and the recent past, reading a person's intent and emotional state; does not see the future, which does not exist to be read. Body — healing and mending flesh, reducing fevers, treating poison, the hedge-witch's daily trade, and contraception through working rather than herb. Hand — telekinesis, the moving of weight without touch, hurling a weapon, lifting a beam, dragging a door or a corpse. These five are the menu of ordinary witch-work and teach them to College students in rotation — a single witch may be expert in one domain or practiced across all five. Sight-trained witches see through glamour that would deceive others. The five domains do not exhaust the tradition; beyond them lie extreme workings taught only to advanced practitioners, paid in mana costs most witches will never accumulate.

The extreme tier.text: The extreme tier: Witchcraft can buy results beyond the five ordinary domains at costs so high that few witches ever attempt them and none would attempt them twice. These extreme workings are not taught in ordinary Queen's College curriculum but are archived in the deep library and given to practitioners ready to pay. Restoration of body — to regrow a lost limb, restore an organ, mend a body the standard work of healing cannot; requires mastery of the Body domain and costs hundreds of mana to work, days of focus to stabilize. Resurrection — to bring back a recently dead person, body reasonably intact, time since death short, the witch fully prepared with the working before death occurred; costs in the thousands of mana and is done perhaps once in a generation even in Avenor. Memory alteration — to rewrite a memory in a living person, false details laid over true ones or true erased from remembrance; costs hundreds of mana and is forbidden outright at the College, a working some call abomination regardless of purpose. Aggregated working at continental scale — many casters pooling their mana-reserves into a single working under a coordinating caster's will, the way Avenoria did. The Ensoulment was thousands of wizards and witches and their stockpiles aggregating to tens of thousands of mana for a single working meant to remake the moral architecture of a continent. Continent-shaping. Done once. Time does not bend in any tradition. True-naming — the art of knowing a thing's true name and commanding it — remains specifically and exclusively Prime ancestor-magic, not a witchcraft tradition or a discipline the Queen's College has ever taught.
